summary: Full review of the Speculator plugin: map subsystems, critique from 4 lenses

maps.summary:
The autonomous pipeline subsystem centers on /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md (230 lines), which keeps the high-level model inline (entry resolution, position detection, trust ladder, headless detection, autonomy modes, self-improvement boundary constraint) and delegates per-phase mechanics to 8 reference files in skills/sdlc-run/references/ (488 lines total). The phase sequence is: Phase 1 Scoring (Gate 1, spec-score skill -> evidence/gate-1-scorecard.yml), Phase 2 Planning (writing-plans skill + beads stories), Phase 2a Eval Authoring (Gate 2a, opt-in via gates.eval-intent.enabled), Phase 3 Implementation + Gate 2 (subagent-driven-development, self-heal loop max max_code_retries=3), Phase 3a Eval Quality (Gate 2b, opt-in, eval-quality-scorer agent, one self-fix cycle), Phase 4 Code Review (Gate 3, code-reviewer agent, one self-fix cycle then escalate), and Phase 5 Evidence Package & Deliver (Gate 4, gate-check evidence-package, beads close, merge-or-PR per close.strategy, then spec-compactor folds the spec into SYSTEM-SPEC.md). Each phase ends with a chore(sdlc) commit, making the pipeline resumable: Section 2's position-detection table re-derives the resume point purely from which evidence YAML files exist in docs/specs/{name}/evidence/, gated by the opt-in flags in .claude/sdlc.local.md.

The trust/autonomy model is a ladder evaluated after Gate 1 scoring (SKILL.md lines 93-112): three hard gates force Guided Autopilot regardless of score (intent_verifiability < intent_verifiability_min (8), risk_level high/critical, scorer risk_mismatch flag); otherwise overall >= self_improvement_trigger (8.0) -> Full Auto skipping self-improvement; below that, a self-improvement loop runs up to max_spec_retries (3), exiting Full Auto at >= full_auto_threshold (7.8), Guided at >= guided_threshold (7.0), or Stopped below 7.0 (creates a P1 spec-rework beads issue and exits without implementing). The loop is bound by an explicit constraint (stated twice, lines 123 and 205): it may add detail/examples/clarifications but must never remove or alter requirements, ACs, scope, or risk_level. Headless detection (CLAUDE_HEADLESS env var, or probing AskUserQuestion failure) changes Guided behavior from interactive pauses (plan approval in Phase 2, merge approval in Phase 5) to commit-work-output-instructions-exit-cleanly. Gates block by escalating with templated human-intervention messages after bounded retry loops; failing evidence is still committed so a follow-up session can resume.

The uncommitted references/phase-comprehension.md (164 lines, git status ??, dated Apr 28 vs the Apr 16 SKILL.md) defines "Phase 3b: Comprehension Gate (Gate 2c)" — an opt-in (gates.comprehension.enabled) anti-dark-code gate that runs between Gate 2b and Gate 3. It dispatches the comprehension-scorer agent (agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md, 290 lines, also untracked) to cold-read the spec + diff (explicitly excluding the implementer's reasoning), generate a per-AC explanation artifact, and score it on 4 dimensions against rubrics/comprehension.md (413 lines, tracked but modified). Failure handling is dimension-aware: artifact-quality failures (AC Coverage, Accuracy, Scope Containment) allow exactly 1 re-dispatch with prior flags as feedback; a Spec Fidelity failure escalates to a human immediately because re-dispatch cannot fix a wrong implementation. On pass, Gate 3's code-reviewer consumes the artifact as preamble. Critically, this phase is wired into nothing committed: sdlc-run/SKILL.md never mentions Phase 3b or comprehension, its position-detection table jumps from Gate 2/2b straight to review, the router skills/sdlc/SKILL.md has no comprehension route, and gate-check/SKILL.md only name-drops Gate 2c once (line 95). README.md line 13 and ROADMAP.md line 45 both describe Gate 2c as "Designed, not yet built — top priority", matching the half-landed working-tree state.

The router skills/sdlc/SKILL.md (230 lines) maps all /sdlc | /spec subcommands to sub-skills, runs a worktree-redirect preamble (via lib/spec-resolution.md) before any spec-bearing command, and inlines two flows itself: /sdlc implement (plan + beads stories + execution-mode handoff) and /sdlc close (Gate 4 + lock release + beads close + merge-or-PR + compaction) — the latter substantially duplicating references/phase-evidence.md, including identical gh pr create steps and PR-body composition, a clear two-sources-of-truth drift risk.
key_facts: Total prompt size (wc -l): sdlc-run/SKILL.md 230 lines; 8 reference files 488 lines (phase-comprehension.md 164, phase-evidence.md 112, phase-scoring.md 62, phase-eval-authoring.md 37, phase-planning.md 33, phase-eval-quality.md 31, phase-implementation.md 25, phase-review.md 24); router sdlc/SKILL.md 230; grand total 948 lines. phase-comprehension.md alone is 34% of all reference content.

Trust ladder defaults (sdlc-run/SKILL.md:95-110 and references/phase-scoring.md run: block): self_improvement_trigger 8.0, full_auto_threshold 7.8, guided_threshold 7.0, max_spec_retries 3, max_code_retries 3, intent_verifiability_min 8. The self-improvement trigger (8.0) is deliberately ABOVE the full-auto threshold (7.8) so even autonomous-eligible specs (7.8-7.9) get one improvement pass — stated as philosophy at SKILL.md:16-21.

Hard gates forcing Guided regardless of score (SKILL.md:96-99): intent_verifiability < min, risk_level high/critical, scorer risk_mismatch flag. Three terminal autonomy modes (SKILL.md:190-199): Full Auto (no pauses), Guided Autopilot (pauses at Phase 2 plan approval + Phase 5 merge approval; in headless: commit + exit with instructions), Stopped (P1 beads issue tagged spec-rework per phase-scoring.md, no implementation started).

Position detection (SKILL.md:64-86) is purely evidence-file driven, checked in order: gate-1-scorecard.yml -> plan in docs/plans/ -> gate-2a-eval-intent.yml (if gates.eval-intent.enabled) -> gate-2-quality.yml -> gate-2b-eval-quality.yml (if gates.eval-quality.enabled) -> gate-3-review.yml -> gate-4-summary.yml -> complete. 'Gate 2a satisfied' accepts result: pass OR override-pass. Resume = re-run /sdlc run; every phase ends with a chore(sdlc) commit checkpoint.

Entry points (SKILL.md:24-60): no args -> resolve active spec via ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/lib/spec-resolution.md (worktree-redirect check first); quoted string -> generate kebab-slug spec skeleton with TODO placeholders + risk_level medium, letting the scorer flag TODOs for the self-improvement loop to flesh out; SPEC-NNN -> grep '^id: SPEC-NNN' across all git worktrees.
Headless detection (SKILL.md:168-187): CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true|1 env var, else probe AskUserQuestion at first Guided checkpoint — if unavailable/error, headless for the rest of the run.

Retry budgets differ per gate: Gate 2 self-heal max_code_retries (3) (phase-implementation.md); Gate 2a max_eval_retries (3, lives in eval-authoring skill); Gate 2b exactly one self-fix cycle (phase-eval-quality.md); Gate 3 exactly one self-fix cycle (phase-review.md); Gate 2c max 1 re-dispatch, artifact-quality dimensions only (phase-comprehension.md:121-122). All exhaustion paths escalate to human with templated messages; failing evidence is committed intentionally (phase-comprehension.md:132-138).

Gate 2c (uncommitted): runs when gates.comprehension.enabled: true, after Gate 2 + 2b, before Gate 3. Evidence: evidence/gate-2c-comprehension.yml. Single-dispatch two-phase agent (generate artifact + score it) at agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md (untracked, 290 lines, model: sonnet). Rubric: rubrics/comprehension.md (tracked, modified, 413 lines; threshold default 7.0, per_dimension_minimum 5). Failure routing splits artifact-quality dims (AC Coverage/Accuracy/Scope Containment -> re-dispatchable) from Spec Fidelity (implementation failure -> immediate human escalation; phase-comprehension.md:94-108). Cold-read constraint requires a fresh subagent so the implementer is never its own judge (phase-comprehension.md:71-80). Interrupted/malformed YAML evidence treated as absent with warning (lines 160-164).

Git state: skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md and agents/comprehension-scorer/ are untracked (??); rubrics/comprehension.md is tracked-but-modified. README.md:13 and ROADMAP.md:45,58 describe Gate 2c as 'Designed, not yet built — top priority'. Plugin version 2.8.0 (.claude-plugin/plugin.json:3).

Router (skills/sdlc/SKILL.md): 13 subcommand routes, /sdlc and /spec equivalent; worktree-awareness preamble before all spec-bearing commands (skipped for start/doctor); /sdlc implement and /sdlc close handled inline rather than delegated; close.strategy merge (default) | pr read from .claude/sdlc.local.md; compaction via spec-compactor agent runs post-merge (merge strategy) or pre-PR on the branch (pr strategy), and a compaction failure warns but never blocks close.

Gate 4 delivery (references/phase-evidence.md): merge strategy = git checkout main && git merge {branch} then compact; pr strategy = compact on branch, verify gh CLI, push, compose PR body from gate evidence with footer 'Quality pipeline: Speculator | Spec: {id} | Score: {score}', worktree stays active until PR merges.
concerns: Gate 2c is dead-on-arrival as written: phase-comprehension.md declares itself 'Phase 3b' executing 'after Gate 2 and Gate 2b... before Gate 3' (lines 1-5), but sdlc-run/SKILL.md contains zero references to it — no Phase 3b section in the phase list (lines 135-164), and position-detection check 4 (SKILL.md:75) routes 'Gate 2 exists (and 2b if enabled), no gate-3-review.yml -> Phase 4: Review' with no comprehension row. An orchestrator following SKILL.md verbatim never reads the file even with gates.comprehension.enabled: true.

phase-comprehension.md:45 and :80 reference an interactive '/sdlc gate comprehension' flow and assert 'the gate-check skill must dispatch a subagent' and (line 155) 'The Gate 3 skill (skills/gate-check/SKILL.md) handles the preamble read' — but gate-check/SKILL.md has no Gate 2c section at all (its only mention is line 95: 'suggest proceeding to Gate 2c (if enabled) or Gate 3'), and the router sdlc/SKILL.md routing table has no comprehension entry. Two claimed integration points do not exist.

SKILL.md:14 says the orchestrator chains 'all five phases — scoring, planning, implementation, review, and merge' and the frontmatter description repeats this, but the same file defines seven phases (1, 2, 2a, 3, 3a, 4, 5) — eight with the pending 3b. Stale count from before the 2a/2b gates were added (v2.7/v2.8 era).

Guided-checkpoint list is incomplete: SKILL.md:184-186 lists only 'Phase 2: Plan approval' and 'Phase 5: Merge approval', but references define more guided pauses — phase-eval-authoring.md step 3 (eval review-before-scoring checkpoint), phase-eval-quality.md step 2.3 ('If in guided mode: stop and ask the user'), and phase-comprehension.md asks the user about re-dispatch in interactive mode. Headless behavior at those undocumented checkpoints is ambiguous.

CLI-name drift: phase-scoring.md, phase-planning.md, and phase-evidence.md hardcode 'beads create', 'beads dep add', 'beads close', 'beads dep list', but sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:129 accepts 'which bd || which beads' and its sample success output (line 163) shows /usr/local/bin/bd. On hosts with only the bd binary (like this machine, bd 1.0.3), the literal commands in the references fail.

Substantial duplication between references/phase-evidence.md and the /sdlc close section of sdlc/SKILL.md (lines 100-209): both contain the full merge-vs-pr strategy logic, identical gh CLI verification, push, PR-body composition, and compaction steps. Step ordering also differs slightly (close: Gate 4 -> release lock -> close beads; phase-evidence: gate-check -> close beads -> remove lock -> commit), so the two will drift independently.

phase-scoring.md 'If Stopped' says to create a beads issue 'tagged spec-rework' but the shown command (beads create --title ... --description ... --priority P1) sets no tag/label, so the tag claim is not implemented by the given command.

Minor key-name shorthand: position-detection table rows (SKILL.md:72,74) write 'eval-intent.enabled: true' / 'eval-quality.enabled: true' while every other reference uses the full 'gates.eval-intent.enabled' path in sdlc.local.md.

Style inconsistency: Phase 3a (SKILL.md:152-156) is the only phase pointing to its reference via absolute '${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/sdlc-run/references/...' and prose, while all other phases use the relative '-> Read references/phase-*.md' arrow format — evidence Phase 3a was patched in later without normalizing.

gates.comprehension.* config defaults live only in the agent and rubric (agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md:128-129, rubrics/comprehension.md:356,393) plus ROADMAP.md:75; the sdlc-doctor config template (which per commit a769bf2 was just updated to surface eval-intent and eval-quality opt-ins) contains no comprehension block, so doctor will not surface the new gate.
opportunities: Wire Phase 3b into sdlc-run/SKILL.md to make the comprehension work shippable: add a position-detection row between checks 3a and 4 (Gate 2 + 2b exist, gates.comprehension.enabled: true, no evidence/gate-2c-comprehension.yml -> Phase 3b), amend check 4's condition to '(and 2b/2c if enabled)', and add a 'Phase 3b: Comprehension (Gate 2c)' entry in the Phases 2-5 list pointing at references/phase-comprehension.md. Then commit phase-comprehension.md + agents/comprehension-scorer/ + the rubrics/comprehension.md modifications together.

Add a 'Gate 2c: Collecting Comprehension Evidence' section to skills/gate-check/SKILL.md (mirroring the existing Gate 2b section at lines 79-96) implementing the interactive '/sdlc gate comprehension' path that phase-comprehension.md:80 already promises, including the mandatory fresh-subagent dispatch for the cold-read constraint and the Gate-3 preamble read promised at phase-comprehension.md:155.

Normalize beads CLI invocations: either standardize on 'bd' (what doctor actually finds) or resolve the binary once at pipeline start ('BEADS_CLI=$(command -v bd || command -v beads)') and reference it in phase-scoring/planning/evidence — matches the doctor's dual-name check at sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:129.

Deduplicate close/delivery logic: have sdlc/SKILL.md's /sdlc close section delegate to references/phase-evidence.md (or extract a shared lib/close-delivery.md), eliminating the parallel-maintained gh-pr/compaction blocks and reconciling the lock-release-vs-beads-close ordering difference.

Fix the stale 'five phases' language in SKILL.md:14 and frontmatter; restate as the gate-count actually implemented (and consider auto-deriving the Guided checkpoint list in section 4 from the per-phase references so new opt-in gates' checkpoints are not silently undocumented).

Add a gates.comprehension block (enabled/threshold 7.0/per_dimension_minimum 5) to the sdlc-doctor config template, continuing the pattern from commit a769bf2 which did exactly this for eval-intent and eval-quality.

The dimension-aware failure routing in phase-comprehension.md (artifact-quality = re-dispatchable vs Spec Fidelity = escalate-only, lines 94-108) is a stronger pattern than the uniform 'one self-fix cycle' in phase-review.md and phase-eval-quality.md — consider back-porting failure-class tables to Gates 2b/3 so the orchestrator distinguishes 'scorer missed something' from 'implementation is wrong'.

phase-comprehension.md:17-19 skips regeneration when a passing artifact exists 'unless the diff has changed' but provides no mechanism to detect diff change; recording the scored diff's hash (git rev-parse of the range endpoints) in gate-2c-comprehension.yml would make the staleness check concrete and applies equally to gate-2 and gate-3 evidence reuse.

maps.summary:
The Speculator gates/rubrics/scorer-agents subsystem is a layered LLM-as-judge quality pipeline: 9 rubric files in /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/rubrics/ define evaluation criteria, and 6 agent prompt files in /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/agents/ (5 tracked + 1 untracked) execute them. Four gates are scored 1-10 with weighted dimensions, thresholds, per-dimension minimums, and blocking flags: Gate 1 Spec Quality (spec-quality.md → spec-scorer, 6 dimensions), opt-in Gate 2a Eval Intent (eval-intent.md → eval-intent-scorer, 4 dimensions), opt-in Gate 2b Eval Quality (eval-quality.md → eval-quality-scorer, 7 dimensions), and the in-development Gate 2c Comprehension (comprehension.md → comprehension-scorer, 4 dimensions). Three gates are binary checklists with no scorer agent by design: Gate 2 Code Quality (code-quality.md + acceptance-criteria.md sub-rubric, executed by the gate-check skill against test/coverage/lint evidence), Gate 3 Review (review.md → code-reviewer agent, 6 pass/fail checks), and Gate 4 Evidence Package (evidence-package.md, mechanical completeness check by gate-check). impact-awareness.md is a non-scoring sub-validation run by spec-scorer after Gate 1 scoring; spec-compactor is the one agent with no rubric (it folds closed specs into SYSTEM-SPEC.md, not a judge).

All scored gates share a uniform design: integer 1-10 per dimension with four calibration bands (1-3 Poor / 4-6 Adequate / 7-8 Good / 9-10 Excellent), inline calibration example snippets per band, a weighted overall rounded to one decimal, and a three-condition pass rule (overall >= threshold AND every dimension >= per-dimension minimum AND zero blocking flags). Anti-gaming is defense-in-depth: explicit Anti-Inflation Guidance sections defining what 7 and 8 mean ("most first drafts should score 5-7"), per-dimension minimums that prevent a strong average masking a weak dimension, blocking flags that force fail regardless of score, an "always include at least one flag" rule in every scorer agent, intent-vs-letter dimensions at three stages (Gate 1 Intent Verifiability, Gate 2b Intent Fidelity, Gate 2c Spec Fidelity — comprehension.md:208 now explicitly disambiguates these), Gate 2c's cold-read principle plus mandatory 3-entry accuracy spot-checks, Gate 3's mandatory regex-driven secrets scan with auto-fail, and impact-awareness "trust-erosion rules" requiring a specifically citable SYSTEM-SPEC.md sentence before a blocking impact_mismatch can be raised.

Rubric/agent sync is achieved primarily by agents reading the rubric at runtime (`${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/rubrics/...`), but two agents also hardcode the weight formulas (eval-intent-scorer Step 5, comprehension-scorer Step B2/B3), creating dual-maintenance risk; spec-scorer and eval-quality-scorer defer entirely to the rubric. The worst drift is Gate 3, where three artifacts describe three different evidence schemas: review.md (6 checks, pass|fail), code-reviewer/AGENT.md (checklist with pass|warn|fail verdicts + notes), and gate-check/SKILL.md (adds a 7th skill_description check that exists nowhere in the rubric or agent, though README credits the agent with it).

The uncommitted work is the Gate 2c buildout: the comprehension.md diff renames "Intent Alignment" → "Spec Fidelity", adds a self-contained artifact-schema section copied from the new agent, adds "What this rubric is NOT" boundary statements, and a naming-disambiguation note vs Gate 1's Intent Verifiability. The untracked comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md is a two-phase agent (Phase A generates the per-AC artifact cold from spec+diff, Phase B scores its own output), and the untracked skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md defines orchestration including re-dispatch-vs-escalate routing (artifact-quality dimension failures may re-dispatch once; Spec Fidelity failures escalate to human since re-running the judge can't fix the implementation). However, nothing executable consumes Gate 2c yet: sdlc-run/SKILL.md's phase list (lines 131-164) never references phase-comprehension.md, and gate-check/SKILL.md only mentions Gate 2c in a one-line suggestion (line 95) with no execution branch — consistent with ROADMAP.md's "Designed, not yet built — top priority" status and the rubric's own DRAFT banner.
key_facts: Gate 1 (rubrics/spec-quality.md, consumed by agents/spec-scorer/AGENT.md): 6 dimensions — Completeness 0.20, Clarity 0.20, Testability 0.20, Intent Verifiability 0.15, Feasibility 0.15, Scope 0.10; threshold from project config (example 7.0), per-dimension minimum 5 (overridable via scoring.per_dimension_minimum); pass = overall>=threshold AND all dims>=min AND no blocking flags (spec-quality.md:273-346)

Gate 2a (rubrics/eval-intent.md, agents/eval-intent-scorer/AGENT.md, opt-in v2.8.0): 4 dimensions — Intent Coverage 0.30, Anti-Pattern Detection 0.25, Journey Completeness 0.25, Implementation Independence 0.20; default threshold 6.5, per-dim min 4; blocking flags include 'eval names a specific function/file' and 'missing coverage for a named anti-pattern' (eval-intent.md:155-205)

Gate 2 (rubrics/code-quality.md, no agent — executed by skills/gate-check/SKILL.md): 7 binary evidence checks gated by config flags (tests_required, coverage_threshold, ac_traceability, build_required, lint_required, type_check_required, plus always-on no-regressions when tests_required); sub-rubric rubrics/acceptance-criteria.md requires 100% AC-to-test coverage with structure+measurability pass

Gate 2b (rubrics/eval-quality.md, agents/eval-quality-scorer/AGENT.md, opt-in v2.7.0): 7 dimensions — AC Coverage 0.25, Behavioral Specificity 0.25, Intent Fidelity 0.20, Sensitivity 0.15, Scenario Completeness 0.10, Assertion Density 0.03, Test Independence 0.02 (sums to 1.00); threshold 6.5, per-dim min 4; dims 6-7 are deterministic counts, dims 2-5 LLM-judged (eval-quality-scorer AGENT.md:50-54)

Gate 2c DRAFT (rubrics/comprehension.md modified-uncommitted, agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md untracked): 4 dimensions — AC Coverage 0.30, Accuracy 0.30, Spec Fidelity 0.25, Scope Containment 0.15; default threshold 7.0, per-dim min 5 — deliberately higher than 2b's 4 with documented rationale (comprehension.md:352-358); evidence path docs/specs/{feature}/evidence/gate-2c-comprehension.yml

The uncommitted comprehension.md diff renames dimension 3 'Intent Alignment' → 'Spec Fidelity' (with disambiguation note vs Gate 1's Intent Verifiability at line 208), embeds the artifact YAML schema 'repeated from agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md so this rubric is self-contained', and adds 'What this rubric is NOT' boundary statements pointing to Gates 1/2b/3

comprehension-scorer is a single-dispatch two-phase agent: Phase A generates the per-AC artifact reading spec+diff cold (ignores implementing agent's reasoning), Phase B scores its own output; mandatory accuracy spot-check of >=3 entries against cited file:line; supports previous_flags re-dispatch input (AGENT.md:53-57, 143-146, 260-262)

Gate 3 (rubrics/review.md, agents/code-reviewer/AGENT.md): 6 binary checks (correctness, error_handling, readability, security, performance, spec_alignment), pass requires all 6 pass + empty blocking_issues; mandatory secrets scan with explicit regex patterns (AWS AKIA, ghp_, xox[bpas]-, sk-, sk-ant-, AIza, private keys, connection strings, bearer tokens) and any true positive = automatic security fail (code-reviewer AGENT.md:39-84)

Gate 4 (rubrics/evidence-package.md, no agent): 6 mechanical checks — gate-1/2/3 evidence files exist with result:pass, no unresolved blocking flags, spec status approved/complete, beads stories closed; only checks gates 1, 2, 3 — never gate-2a/2b/2c evidence even when those opt-in gates are enabled

impact-awareness.md is a separate non-scoring validation run by spec-scorer after the 6 dimensions: decision matrix maps detected SYSTEM-SPEC.md domain overlap × declared impact_rating × amends field to flags impact_mismatch (blocking, requires citable specific behavior entry), impact_underspecified / impact_incomplete (recommended), system_spec_malformed (advisory)

Anti-gaming stack common to scored gates: Anti-Inflation Guidance sections ('most first drafts score 5-7', 9-10 rare), per-dimension minimums, blocking-flag-forces-fail, 'always include at least one flag' rule in all four scorer agents, integer-only scores, weighted overall to 1 decimal

Calibration anchors: every scored dimension in spec-quality.md, eval-intent.md, eval-quality.md, comprehension.md has example snippets at bands 2-3 / 5-6 / 7-8 / 9-10; comprehension.md:404-413 additionally specifies production-readiness requirements (10-15 examples per dimension per band, >=5 plausible-but-wrong Accuracy cases, >=3 letter-vs-spirit Spec Fidelity cases, spec-bench calibrate divergence >1 point → needs_tuning)

Orchestration wiring: sdlc-run/SKILL.md trust ladder uses self_improvement_trigger 8.0 > full_auto_threshold 7.8 > guided_threshold 7.0, with hard gates (intent_verifiability below min, risk_level high/critical, risk_mismatch flag) forcing Guided mode; phase reference files dispatch agents per phase

Gate 2c failure routing in untracked skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md: AC Coverage/Accuracy/Scope Containment failures = artifact-quality (re-dispatch once with previous flags as feedback); Spec Fidelity failure = implementation problem (escalate to human, re-dispatch will NOT help); fail evidence is committed intentionally

Tracked file counts: 9 rubrics, 5 agents (git ls-files); plugin version 2.8.0 (.claude-plugin/plugin.json); comprehension.md was committed in f72bb03 'Gate 2c rubric draft' and has uncommitted modifications; comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md and phase-comprehension.md are untracked
concerns: Gate 2c is half-wired and entirely unexecutable: rubrics/comprehension.md (tracked, modified) and agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md (untracked) exist, but no skill dispatches them — sdlc-run/SKILL.md's phase list (lines 131-164) references phase-scoring/planning/eval-authoring/implementation/eval-quality/review/evidence but never phase-comprehension.md, and gate-check/SKILL.md's only Gate 2c content is the suggestion line 95 ('suggest proceeding to Gate 2c (if enabled)') despite phase-comprehension.md:80 asserting 'From interactive /sdlc gate comprehension, the gate-check skill must dispatch a subagent' — that gate-check branch does not exist

Three-way Gate 3 schema drift: rubrics/review.md:137-143 specifies checks: {6 items} with pass|fail; agents/code-reviewer/AGENT.md:157-179 outputs checklist: {verdict: pass|warn|fail, notes} (warn tier absent from rubric); skills/gate-check/SKILL.md:125-134 expects a 7th check skill_description: pass|fail|skipped that neither the rubric nor the agent knows about. README.md:472 claims code-reviewer does 'skill-description eval' but the AGENT.md contains no such step — evidence files will vary in shape depending on which path produced them

Weight duplication between rubric and agent: eval-intent-scorer/AGENT.md:78 hardcodes 'intent_coverage×0.30 + anti_pattern_detection×0.25 + journey_completeness×0.25 + implementation_independence×0.20' and comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md:136-141,158 hardcodes its weight table — both duplicate the rubric's weights, so a rubric weight change silently desyncs the agent (spec-scorer and eval-quality-scorer correctly defer to rubric+config)

Gate 4 (evidence-package.md) predates the opt-in gates: it verifies only gate-1-scorecard.yml, gate-2-quality.yml, gate-3-review.yml — a project with gates.eval-intent.enabled or gates.eval-quality.enabled can pass Gate 4 with missing/failed gate-2a/gate-2b evidence; same gap will apply to gate-2c when it ships

README.md:469/477 says '5 agents' and '8 rubrics' but the tracked tree has 9 rubrics (comprehension.md committed in f72bb03) and the working tree has 6 agents — the structure listing omits comprehension.md and comprehension-scorer entirely

comprehension.md carries an explicit DRAFT banner (line 5): 'not production-ready until each dimension has 10-15 calibrated examples, with explicit plausible-but-wrong cases for Accuracy' — current calibration is 1 seed example per band, far short of its own stated bar

The comprehension.md weights table at line 347 has a formatting remnant from the rename ('| Spec Fidelity   | 0.25   |' with misaligned padding) — cosmetic but signals the find-and-replace nature of the uncommitted edit

Intentional schema duplication inside the Gate 2c pair: comprehension.md:9 repeats the artifact YAML 'so this rubric is self-contained' from comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md:191-243 — two copies of the same schema in two uncommitted-state files; any artifact-shape change must be made twice (the rubric at least declares the duplication)

Gate 2b dimension weights make Assertion Density (0.03) and Test Independence (0.02) nearly irrelevant to the overall score — they only bite via the per-dimension minimum of 4; whether that is intended de-emphasis or vestigial is undocumented

maps.summary:
Speculator's interactive command surface is 9 prose-only skills (no executable code — every skill is an LLM instruction document): a master router (`skills/sdlc/SKILL.md`, 230 lines) that maps `/sdlc|/spec {start,score,gate,eval,status,implement,review,close,run,compact,doctor}` to sub-skills, plus 7 in-scope sub-skills (spec-create, spec-score, eval-authoring, gate-check, spec-compact, sdlc-status, sdlc-doctor) and the autonomous sdlc-run orchestrator. Two commands — `/sdlc implement` and `/sdlc close` — have no dedicated skill; their full procedures live inline in the master router (sdlc/SKILL.md lines 47-99 and 100-209), even though the routing table claims close just invokes gate-check. Shared logic is factored into `lib/spec-resolution.md` (worktree redirect check, 5-step spec resolution order, `.active` lock lifecycle) and two templates (`spec-template.md` with impact_rating/amends frontmatter for SYSTEM-SPEC awareness; `scorecard-template.yml` for Gate 1 output).

The user journey: `/sdlc start` runs sdlc-doctor, reads `.claude/sdlc.local.md`, feeds `{spec_dir}/SYSTEM-SPEC.md` as drafting context, creates a git worktree via the EnterWorktree tool, symlinks the worktree's Claude Code memory dir to main's (sanitized-path sed hack), copies the spec template, writes a `.active` lock, and creates a beads epic. `/sdlc score` dispatches the spec-scorer agent against a 6-dimension rubric (threshold 7.0, per-dimension minimum 5), flipping frontmatter `status: draft → approved`. `/sdlc implement` builds a plan (superpowers `writing-plans` if available) and beads stories linked to the epic. Optional Gate 2a (`/sdlc eval`, eval-authoring skill) authors one intent eval per AC with resume support via an `evidence/.eval-session-partial` marker, scored by eval-intent-scorer with a retry/override loop. `/sdlc gate` checks/collects evidence YAMLs (gate-1-scorecard, gate-2-quality with 7 configurable checks, optional gate-2b-eval-quality, gate-3-review including a conditional skill-description trigger check, gate-4-summary). `/sdlc close` runs Gate 4, releases the lock, closes beads issues, delivers via `close.strategy: merge|pr`, then invokes spec-compactor to fold the spec into SYSTEM-SPEC.md (`status: closed → compacted`). State is entirely file-reconstructed: no central state file — pipeline position is inferred from which evidence YAMLs exist (sdlc-run's position-detection table), worktree affinity is by directory-name == spec-name convention, and concurrency is handled by gitignored `.active` lock files with stale-lock detection in sdlc-doctor.

Configuration is a single YAML-frontmatter file, `.claude/sdlc.local.md`, generated by `/sdlc doctor --init` (full default template embedded in sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md lines 193-278): spec_dir, evidence_dir, per-gate config (4 required gates + 2 opt-in eval gates), scoring weights summing to 1.0, close.strategy, and `/sdlc run` autonomy thresholds (self_improvement_trigger 8.0 > full_auto_threshold 7.8 > guided_threshold 7.0 — deliberate "refinement as practice" design). The speculator repo dog-foods itself: its own `.claude/sdlc.local.md` enables Gate 2a (eval-intent, threshold 6.5) but not Gate 2b, and uses `close.strategy: pr`. However the dog-food state shows drift: the repo's SYSTEM-SPEC.md sits at the repo root while every skill looks for it at `{spec_dir}/SYSTEM-SPEC.md` (docs/specs/SYSTEM-SPEC.md does not exist), and `spec_dir` configurability is only half-honored — lib/spec-resolution.md, sdlc-status's worktree scan, sdlc-doctor's checks, sdlc-run's SPEC-ID search, eval-authoring's write paths, and the .gitignore lock pattern all hardcode `docs/specs/`.

Trust ladder (sdlc-run/SKILL.md lines 93-112): hard gates (intent_verifiability < 8, risk_level high/critical, risk_mismatch flag) force Guided; otherwise overall >= 8.0 → Full Auto skipping self-improvement; < 8.0 → self-improvement loop (max 3), then >= 7.8 Full Auto, >= 7.0 Guided, else Stopped. maps.summary:
Speculator (/Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator) is a Claude Code plugin at v2.8.0 implementing a spec-driven SDLC quality pipeline. Its documentation layer is unusually deliberate and mostly coherent: README.md (594 lines) describes a 6-stage gate pipeline (Gates 1/2/3/4 required, 2a eval-intent and 2b eval-quality opt-in), a trust-ladder autonomous orchestrator (/spec run), SYSTEM-SPEC.md living-spec compaction, worktree isolation, beads integration, and the Spec-Bench benchmark harness. MANIFESTO.md (202 lines) is the thesis document ("Quality In, Quality Out" — nobody measures spec quality; Speculator is the missing measure), extended with an anti-dark-code section (VI½) built on Nate B Jones's Apr 2026 "dark code" framing. ROADMAP.md (232 lines, "Last updated 2026-04-28") declares Gate 2c (Comprehension Gate) the #1 priority, with a status table marking everything else shipped. CHANGELOG.md covers 9 versions (1.0.0→2.8.0 with gaps). docs/ contains only an honest self-review (REVIEW-2026-04-28.md) and one dogfooded spec (docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring, SPEC-001, status: compacted, all 4 gate evidence files present). All headline test claims verify live: 30/30 eval-intent structure, 25/25 secrets-scan, 67 benchmark pytest tests collected.

The most significant doc-vs-reality gap is Gate 2c. maps.summary:
Spec-Bench (`/Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/benchmarks/`) is a Python harness (package `spec_bench`, ~3,185 LOC across 11 modules, uv/hatchling, Python >=3.12) that tests Speculator's core thesis: spec quality predicts implementation quality. The automated pipeline (cli.py `run` command) has 4 phases: (1) each matrix target generates a spec from a PRD via bash adapters (`adapters/claude-code.sh`, `adapters/copilot-cli.sh`); (2) `scoring.py` scores it with `claude -p` against an inlined 6-dimension rubric (threshold 7.8, max 3 feedback iterations, best version saved as spec-improved.md); (3) a constant implementer (claude-code + sonnet + superpowers) builds the app twice — from spec-v0 and spec-improved — to isolate spec quality as the variable; (4) `review.py` runs an "Option C" hybrid functional layer (Playwright + deterministic API mocks in `mock_data.py` + LLM vision judge in `vision_judge.py`, with programmatic localStorage/responsive checks overriding LLM verdicts) plus an outcome LLM-as-judge (opus-4-6) against `rubrics/outcome-rubric.md`. There is also a human-in-the-loop judge-calibration protocol (`calibration.py`) and a report generator with pearson correlation machinery (`report.py`). 67 unit tests all pass.

The famous "18-point result" (`runs/3-round-spec-quality/results.yml`, committed copy at `results/3-round-spec-quality.yml`) did NOT come from this harness. Its own header says "Methodology: Manual 3-round benchmark". The 18 points are 3 models (sonnet-4-6, opus-4-6, gpt-5.4) x 3 rounds x 2 stages (raw spec score, score after one feedback iteration) — spec scores only, no implementations, no outcome measurement. Findings F1-F6 (e.g., "Speculator ALWAYS improves specs, 9/9 positive lift"; "all models converge to 8.4-8.6"; "a $1 model + Speculator matches a $3 model") are claims about judge-score behavior, not about shipped software quality. The raw specs and scorecards from that run were not preserved — only the summary YAML exists — so it is unreproducible and unauditable. The harness can't even run one of its three models: gpt-5.4 has no adapter (the matrix offers gpt-4.1 via copilot-cli).

Methodologically the iteration-lift measurement is self-referential: `build_feedback_prompt` (scoring.py:246) constructs the improvement prompt directly from the scorer's own flags, then the same scorer re-scores — a Goodhart loop where "always improves" partly means "the judge rewards compliance with its own feedback." There is no control arm (e.g., regenerate without feedback) to separate feedback value from extra-pass compute. The scorer model is not pinned: `score_spec` calls `claude -p` with no `--model` flag (scoring.py:196-201), so the claimed "scorer_model: claude-sonnet-4-6" depends on the operator's CLI default. Sigmas are computed over n=3. The only end-to-end harness run ever (`runs/bench-2026-03-28-001`, single target cc-vanilla-sonnet smoke) actually undermines the thesis: spec score improved 7.0 -> 8.3 over 2 iterations, but functional-results.yml is byte-identical for original and improved (12/20 both), the error messages are misaligned with their requirements (evidence it was produced by the old broken selector-based runner that Option C replaced), and the improved implementation has no judge-scorecard.yml at all. Token metrics are dead (metrics.json reports 0 tokens; `iteration_tokens` in scoring.py is initialized and never incremented; the adapters' `grep "input tokens"` doesn't match `claude -p` output).

Artifact state: the harness is reusable in design but one-shot in practice — the rebuilt Option C functional layer has never produced a recorded full run, the 8-target default matrix was never executed, and calibration was never run (no calibration/ artifacts anywhere). `weather-transport-app/` is committed cruft: its only content is `.vite/deps_temp_2247e353/package.json` ({"type":"module"}), a Vite temp file committed in ceee8b2 "feat: Smart Commuter complete implementation". Worse, 45 files of generated benchmark app source are tracked under `runs/bench-2026-03-28-001/implementations/cc-vanilla-sonnet-original/app/` despite `runs/` being gitignored — 52 commits touch benchmarks/runs/, because the benchmark's constant implementer (running with --dangerously-skip-permissions inside the speculator working tree) committed directly to the host repo during the run. The Remotion video project (`spec-bench-video/`, 592MB incl. rendered out/spec-bench.mp4) and `benchmarks/docs/` (containing the only copy of the Option C plan) are entirely gitignored/local-only.

critiques.assessment:
Speculator's true user is visible in the artifacts: a solo developer on a Max subscription who wants to let agents build features unattended and needs an evidence trail to trust the result. For that user, the core design is genuinely strong — the trust ladder (verified at skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:93-112), evidence-file-driven resume (re-run /sdlc run and position is re-derived from which YAMLs exist), committed failing evidence, and a single well-documented config file (.claude/sdlc.local.md, template embedded in sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:193-278) are exactly the right shape for autonomous-with-receipts development. The product's marketing (README's "scale across multiple agents", MANIFESTO's public-benchmark framing) aims at a broader audience the repo can't yet serve: dogfood shows one spec ever completed, no CI, no tags, and a hard dependency on a niche fork of an issue tracker (beads) that doctor treats as FAIL-level required.

The interactive surface is where cost exceeds benefit for the actual user. The happy path in README's Quick Start is seven interactive steps per feature before counting the optional eval phase, and every scored gate is an LLM-as-judge subagent dispatch reading a 300-400-line rubric plus spec plus diff: a full-auto run with both opt-in gates enabled can legitimately reach ~10-14 judge/implementer dispatches (up to 4 spec-scorer calls in the self-improvement loop, up to 4 eval-intent-scorer calls, one implementation subagent per plan task, up to 3 Gate 2 self-heal cycles, 2 eval-quality-scorer, 2 code-reviewer, 1 spec-compactor). Yet there is zero cost or wall-clock guidance anywhere in user-facing docs (verified by grep across README.md, ROADMAP.md, sdlc-run/SKILL.md — the only latency acknowledgment is ROADMAP.md:110 about the unshipped Gate 2c). All judges run sonnet (verified in all 6 AGENT.md frontmatters), which is the right cost choice, but a user has no way to predict whether /spec run on a small feature will take 10 minutes or 90, or what it does to their session budget. The opt-in gate design (2a/2b off by default) is the correct lever; it just isn't framed as a cost model.

The sharpest product problem today is that the first ten minutes of a new user's experience are broken: the installed plugin self-reports version 2.7.0 inside a directory named 2.8.0 (verified directly in ~/.claude/plugins/cache), the installed doctor emits an outdated config template, README promises `/spec doctor --init` registers the pre-commit hook (it doesn't — only --fix does, via a stale settings.local.json mechanism), and doctor's plugin-wiring check greps for a hook that actually ships auto-registered via hooks/hooks.json — so a perfectly healthy fresh install gets a perpetual WARN. Meanwhile the README quickstart references a `/spec create` step that has no row in the router's routing table, and skills hardcode the `beads` CLI name while doctor accepts `bd` — meaning doctor passes and then the first scripted command fails. None of these are deep flaws; all of them are first-session trust killers for exactly the marketplace stranger the README courts. Eleven routes is a defensible surface; an inconsistent surface is not.

findings.title: Marketplace install path is broken: version lie, stale doctor template, false-WARN wiring check, and README --init overpromise
findings.evidence: Verified directly: ~/.claude/plugins/cache/dmokong-plugins/speculator/2.8.0/.claude-plugin/plugin.json contains "version": "2.7.0" with the old "4-gate pipeline" description. Repo sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:47-51 greps .claude/settings.local.json for 'SDLC PRE-COMMIT' (the hook actually ships via hooks/hooks.json, so healthy installs WARN forever). README.md (Installation section) claims '/spec doctor --init will ... register the pre-commit hook' but the --init procedure (sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:188-281) never mentions hooks; only --fix does, targeting the stale settings.local.json mechanism. Installed cache doctor also lacks the opt-in gate blocks and 6-dimension weights present in repo sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:216-237.
findings.detail: Onboarding is the single highest-stakes DX surface for a marketplace plugin, and every step of it currently misfires: the plugin reports the wrong version, doctor warns about a non-problem on a healthy install (training users to ignore warnings), the documented bootstrap promise is false, and any project initialized from the installed plugin gets a config schema the current skills no longer match. A 2.8.1 re-release through the three-step process plus a doctor fix closes nearly all of this in one pass.

findings.title: No cost/wall-clock model for a pipeline that is wall-to-wall LLM-judge dispatches
findings.evidence: grep for wall/token/cost/minutes/expensive/budget across README.md, ROADMAP.md, and skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md returns only ROADMAP.md:110 (latency note on unshipped Gate 2c) and unrelated hits. Dispatch budget per full-auto run derived from skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:100-110 (max_spec_retries 3 → up to 4 spec-scorer calls), eval-authoring/SKILL.md (max_eval_retries 3 → up to 4 eval-intent-scorer calls), references/phase-implementation.md (subagent per plan task + 3 self-heal cycles), phase-eval-quality.md and phase-review.md (2 dispatches each), plus spec-compactor. All 6 agents declare model: sonnet (verified in agents/*/AGENT.md frontmatter).
findings.detail: For a solo dev on Max, the cost is session/rate-limit budget and wall-clock, not dollars — and neither is documented anywhere. A user cannot predict whether /spec run takes 10 or 90 minutes, nor reason about when the gate overhead exceeds the value for a small change. The mitigating levers already exist (opt-in gates off by default, sonnet judges, bounded retries) but are never framed as a cost model. ROADMAP's own idea — bind expensive gates to risk_level rather than a global flag — is proposed only for Gate 2c and should be the general design. A 'pipeline cost' README section (typical dispatches and duration per phase, plus a documented lite profile) is cheap and high-leverage.

findings.title: Command surface (11 subcommands) is defensible but internally dishonest: routing table misrepresents close, /spec create exists only in README and skill description, implement/close live inline in the router
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc/SKILL.md:32-45 routing table: 'close' row says 'Run Gate 4 — invoke gate-check skill with gate=evidence-package' while the real close flow is the 110-line inline section at lines 100-209 (lock release, beads closure, merge-vs-pr delivery, compaction). README Quick Start shows a '/spec create' step ('Speculator turns your brainstormed plan into a formal spec.md') but the routing table has no create row; spec-create/SKILL.md:6 claims the trigger. /sdlc implement is also fully inline (lines 47-99), duplicating planning/story logic in references/phase-planning.md and delivery logic in references/phase-evidence.md.
findings.detail: The route count is not the problem — start/score/eval/gate/implement/review/close/run plus status/doctor/compact is a coherent verb set for the pipeline's shape. The problem is that the three descriptions of the surface (routing table, README quickstart, inline implementations) disagree, so a new user following the README types a command the router doesn't list, and a maintainer or sub-agent reading only the table skips most of what close does. Extracting implement/close into dedicated skills (matching what the table already implies) and adding the create alias to the table makes the surface self-consistent without changing its size. Minor redundancy worth noting: 'review' ≈ 'gate review' and no-args ≈ 'status' — acceptable aliases if documented as such.

findings.title: Failure UX is inconsistent across gates: override exists only for Gate 2a, retry budgets differ without rationale, Gate 1 hard-blocks with no escape hatch
findings.evidence: Override-with-justification (recorded as result: override-pass) exists only in eval-authoring/SKILL.md steps 5-7; grep for 'override' in spec-score/SKILL.md, gate-check/SKILL.md, and sdlc-run/ (excluding eval-intent) returns nothing. Retry budgets: Gate 2 self-heal 3 (references/phase-implementation.md), Gate 2a 3 (eval-authoring), Gate 2b exactly 1 (phase-eval-quality.md), Gate 3 exactly 1 (phase-review.md). In /sdlc run, a spec scoring below guided_threshold 7.0 after 3 self-improvement passes → Stopped, P1 spec-rework beads issue, no implementation (sdlc-run/SKILL.md:105-110, phase-scoring.md).
findings.detail: The good parts are genuinely good: failing evidence is committed so a follow-up session resumes cleanly, escalation messages are templated, the .eval-session-partial marker gives Gate 2a true mid-phase resume, and 'resume = re-run /sdlc run' is excellent DX (though buried — README never advertises it). But a solo developer who IS the project owner cannot override their own Gate 1 at 6.9/7.0 — the judged party has fewer rights than at Gate 2a, where override-with-recorded-justification was already designed and shipped. One documented policy table (retries, override availability, escalation) applied uniformly would make gate failure predictable instead of gate-specific lore. The differing 3/3/1/1 retry budgets may be intentional (judge-fixable vs implementation-fixable) but nothing says so.

findings.title: sdlc-status — the primary 'where am I' surface — is blind to the opt-in gates, and Gate 4 never verifies their evidence
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc-status/SKILL.md (64 lines, read in full) displays only Gates 1/2/3/4 and contains zero occurrences of eval-intent, eval-quality, 2a, or 2b; its display example jumps Gate 2 → Gate 3. rubrics/evidence-package.md checks only gate-1/2/3 evidence files (per gates-rubrics reader, consistent with gate-check/SKILL.md step list). Speculator's own .claude/sdlc.local.md enables eval-intent, so the dogfood repo itself gets misleading status output.
findings.detail: When Gate 2a is enabled, status tells the user the next blocker is Gate 2 review when the actual next blocker is eval authoring — the one command whose entire job is orientation gives a wrong answer for any project using the v2.7/v2.8 flagship features. Compounding it, Gate 4 will happily pass and close a spec whose enabled opt-in gates never ran, silently voiding the gates a user explicitly turned on. Both fixes are small (read gates.*.enabled from config, add conditional rows/checks) and both directly affect whether users can trust the pipeline's self-reporting — the product's entire value proposition.

findings.title: Configuration surface contains dead and half-wired knobs: evidence_dir is never read, spec_dir is hardcoded in 6+ places, and the dogfood repo's own SYSTEM-SPEC.md sits where no skill looks
findings.evidence: evidence_dir appears in the --init template (sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:196) but no skill/lib/agent reads it (skills-ux reader grep; consistent with gate-check/SKILL.md hardcoding evidence/ paths I read). docs/specs/ is hardcoded in lib/spec-resolution.md, sdlc-status step 3b (verified, lines 25-31), sdlc-doctor section 4 (verified, line 59), sdlc/SKILL.md:111 close step, eval-authoring paths. Speculator's SYSTEM-SPEC.md is at repo root while skills resolve {spec_dir}/SYSTEM-SPEC.md (spec-create:33, eval-authoring step 2, sdlc close:142) — docs/specs/SYSTEM-SPEC.md does not exist.
findings.detail: The single-file config design is one of the product's best DX decisions — thresholds, weights, opt-in gates, close strategy, and autonomy knobs in one documented place. But knobs that do nothing are worse than no knobs: a user who sets spec_dir or evidence_dir gets breakage or silent no-ops with no warning from doctor. And the flagship 'living spec' feature is silently disabled in the maker's own repo because of the path mismatch — meaning the impact-awareness and amends machinery the docs showcase has effectively never run where users would look first for proof it works. Either thread the config values through (read once, substitute everywhere) or delete them from the template.

findings.title: Hard beads dependency plus bd/beads CLI-name drift creates a 'doctor passes, first command fails' onboarding sequence and is the biggest external adoption filter
findings.evidence: sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:126-133: section 6 checks `which bd || which beads` and rates missing CLI as FAIL ('required for SDLC issue tracking'). But skills hardcode the `beads` name: sdlc/SKILL.md:71-75 (beads create / beads dep add), :119-121 (beads dep list / beads close), spec-create, and sdlc-run references phase-scoring/planning/evidence. README Prerequisites lists beads CLI as required (`npm install -g beads-cli`). On a bd-only host (like this machine, bd 1.0.3), doctor passes via the `which bd` branch and every scripted command then fails.
findings.detail: For the product's actual user (the author), this is a latent footgun; for the marketplace audience the README courts, requiring a niche issue tracker just to score a spec is the first thing that turns a curious installer away. The pipeline's core value (scoring, gates, evidence, autonomous runs) is functionally independent of issue tracking — beads provides traceability garnish. Downgrading the doctor check to WARN with a documented degraded mode (no epics/stories), and resolving the binary name once (BEADS_CLI=$(command -v bd || command -v beads)) referenced by all skills, removes both the adoption filter and the inconsistency.

findings.title: Positioning mismatch: marketed as multi-agent/team infrastructure, but every artifact says 'solo author's personal pipeline' — and the docs never name the real persona
findings.evidence: README.md: 'designed to scale across multiple agents. Each agent gets a brainstormed plan, runs /spec run in its own worktree'; MANIFESTO.md positions a community benchmark. Reality: one completed spec ever (docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring), v2.8.0's flagship feature dogfooded in a different repo (SYSTEM-SPEC.md is 35 lines, SPEC-001 only), no CI, no git tags, install base of one with a stale cache, prerequisites pointing at a personal beads fork (github.com/DMokong/beads).
findings.detail: The product's strongest, most honest pitch — 'guardrails that let one developer trust unattended agent runs, with an evidence trail instead of vibes' — is exactly what the trust ladder, committed evidence, and resume-by-rerun deliver. The current framing oversells team/community scale the infrastructure can't yet support (no CI, no release process doc, single-PRD benchmark) while underselling the solo-autonomy story it has actually proven. Naming the persona would also resolve design tensions concretely: a solo dev needs the lite/risk-conditional gate profile and self-override rights; a team needs the override-audit-trail and PR strategy. Right now the surface splits the difference.

findings.title: Best-in-class DX features exist but are undiscoverable: resume-by-rerun, partial eval sessions, and headless mode are documented only inside skill internals
findings.evidence: Position-detection/resume: skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:64-86 ('Resume = re-run /sdlc run'). Mid-phase eval resume: eval-authoring/SKILL.md step 1.4 (.eval-session-partial marker). Headless detection: sdlc-run/SKILL.md:168-187 (CLAUDE_HEADLESS env var, AskUserQuestion probe, commit-and-exit-with-instructions behavior). README.md mentions none of these in its Quick Start or feature list (grep for 'resume', 'headless', 'CLAUDE_HEADLESS' in README returns nothing in user-facing sections).
findings.detail: These three mechanisms are the answer to the most common anxiety about autonomous pipelines — 'what happens when it stops halfway?' — and they are genuinely well designed: every phase commits a checkpoint, position is reconstructed purely from evidence files, guided checkpoints degrade gracefully to commit-work-and-exit in headless contexts. A user evaluating the plugin from the README has no idea any of this exists. A short 'Interruption & resume' README section would convert the architecture's best property into a selling point at zero implementation cost. (Caveat from the pipeline reader, consistent with my read of eval-authoring step 8: the Guided checkpoint list at SKILL.md:184-186 omits the eval-review and eval-quality pauses, so headless behavior at those checkpoints is currently ambiguous — worth fixing in the same pass.)

top_recommendations: Fix the install path first: cut a 2.8.1 re-release (correct plugin.json version, current doctor config template), change sdlc-doctor's plugin-wiring check to verify hooks/hooks.json instead of grepping settings.local.json, and align the README --init claim — the new-user first session currently includes a version lie and a guaranteed false warning.

Publish a cost/latency model and a documented 'lite profile': a README section stating typical judge dispatches and wall-clock per phase, plus a minimal-gates configuration for small changes, and generalize ROADMAP's risk_level-conditional gate enablement beyond Gate 2c — today a Max-subscription user has no way to reason about what /spec run costs.

Make the pipeline's self-reporting honest: teach sdlc-status the opt-in gates (read gates.*.enabled, add 2a/2b rows), extend Gate 4/evidence-package to require enabled opt-in gate evidence, and fix the routing table's misdescription of /sdlc close (ideally extracting implement/close into the sub-skills the table already implies).

Unify failure UX with one documented policy: a single retries/override/escalation table across gates, extending Gate 2a's override-with-recorded-justification pattern (or explicitly justifying its absence) so a 6.9/7.0 Gate 1 score isn't a dead end for the project's own author — and advertise resume-by-rerun, partial eval sessions, and headless mode in the README.

Soften the beads dependency to WARN/degraded mode and resolve the bd/beads binary name once at pipeline start — it is simultaneously the biggest external adoption filter and a doctor-passes-then-commands-fail trap on bd-only hosts.

critiques.assessment:
Speculator's prompt-as-code architecture is genuinely well-designed at the pattern level: a thin router, an orchestrator that keeps the conceptual model (trust ladder, position detection, self-improvement boundary) inline at 230 lines while delegating per-phase mechanics to 8 reference files read progressively, scorer agents dispatched as cold-context subagents, and pipeline state reconstructed purely from evidence YAMLs (making every run resumable with no state file). The context budget is also healthier than the file counts suggest — always-loaded frontmatter is only ~4.3KB across 9 skills + ~2.6KB across 6 agents, and the references/ pattern means a phase costs ~25-165 lines of incremental load rather than the whole 948-line pipeline corpus (worst-case full read ≈ 48.5KB). The main budget violation is the router itself: /sdlc implement and /sdlc close are implemented inline (sdlc/SKILL.md:47-209, ~160 lines), so every /sdlc invocation loads two full sub-command implementations it usually doesn't need, and the routing table at line 41 actively misdescribes close as "invoke gate-check."

The structural weakness is that prose has no compiler, and at v2.8.0 with 6 gates the cross-file contracts are already drifting measurably. Adding a gate requires ~10-12 coordinated edits (rubric, agent, phase reference, position-detection row, orchestrator phase section, gate-check section, doctor config template, sdlc-status display, evidence-package rubric, README, CHANGELOG/version, structural test), and the observed completion rate decreases with each gate: Gate 2b shipped without sdlc-status or Gate-4 integration; Gate 2a additionally left its guided checkpoint out of the headless checkpoint list; Gate 2c (the next gate) stalled entirely — ~870 lines of mutually-coherent rubric/agent/phase-reference sitting untracked for 45 days with zero wiring in any committed skill and one dead pointer (gate-check:95) already shipped in production. Gate 4's evidence-package rubric still only verifies gates 1/2/3, so the opt-in gates can be enabled, fail, and the pipeline still "passes." Meanwhile the three-step manual release has concretely failed: the installed cache directory is named 2.8.0 and installed_plugins.json records 2.8.0, but the cached plugin.json says 2.7.0 because the "bump version to 2.8.0" commit (6703adc) never edited plugin.json; the real bump (f72bb03) was never re-released, there are zero git tags, and the marketplace clone is a commit behind its own origin.

Verdict on absorbing the next 3 gates: the rubric+agent+phase-reference unit pattern scales fine — each new gate is additive and self-contained. The wiring does not scale as practiced. Three more gates means a ~10-row position table with an already-strained naming scheme (2a after phase 2, 3a after phase 3, 3b proposed), ~9 gate-check sections, and an evidence-package rubric falling further behind — collapse looks like Gate 2c repeated: complete components, orphaned integration, drifting duplicates. The saving grace is that the project already invented its own fix and used it exactly once: tests/test-eval-intent-structure.sh is a 30-assertion structural compiler for Gate 2a's wiring. Templating that per gate, plus a release-consistency check and de-duplicating the close flow, converts the architecture from "maintained by vigilance" to "maintained by mechanism" at near-zero runtime cost.

findings.title: Three-step manual release has already failed silently: installed plugin self-reports 2.7.0 inside a 2.8.0-named cache
findings.evidence: ~/.claude/plugins/cache/dmokong-plugins/speculator/2.8.0/.claude-plugin/plugin.json contains "version": "2.7.0" with the old "4-gate pipeline" description; `git show 6703adc:.claude-plugin/plugin.json` (the commit titled 'chore: bump version to 2.8.0') still says 2.7.0 — the real bump is in f72bb03, never re-released. installed_plugins.json records version 2.8.0 / gitCommitSha 6703adc. Marketplace clone ~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/dmokong-plugins @ b56b833 is one commit behind ~/projects/claude-plugins @ 472b149. `git tag` in /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator returns empty.
findings.detail: The release process (plugin repo -> marketplace repo -> local cache copy) has no verification step, so a bump commit that forgot to edit plugin.json shipped and nobody noticed for ~8 weeks. Consequence beyond the version string: the cached skills/sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md emits the old 4-dimension 0.25-each Gate 1 config template with no opt-in gate blocks, so any project doctored via the installed plugin gets an outdated config schema. With zero git tags, release archaeology requires `git log -p` on plugin.json. A 5-line shell check (plugin.json == CHANGELOG top == marketplace.json) run pre-release would have caught this.

findings.title: Gate 2c proves the per-gate integration cost is no longer being paid: ~870 lines of complete components, zero wiring, 45 days untracked
findings.evidence: Untracked: agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md (290 lines), skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md (164 lines); tracked-but-modified: rubrics/comprehension.md (413 lines). `grep -rni 'comprehension|2c|3b'` across committed skills returns exactly one hit: gate-check/SKILL.md:95 ('suggest proceeding to Gate 2c (if enabled)'). sdlc-run/SKILL.md position table (lines 68-77) has rows 1,2,2a,3,3a,4,5 — no 3b; phase list jumps 3a (line 152) to 4 (line 158). phase-comprehension.md:80 asserts 'the gate-check skill must dispatch a subagent' and lines 89-90 assert code-reviewer reads the artifact as preamble — neither integration point exists in any committed file. Doctor config template (sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:193-278) has no gates.comprehension block.
findings.detail: The three Gate 2c files are internally coherent (weights 0.30/0.30/0.25/0.15, threshold 7.0, per-dim min 5, evidence path all agree), so the component pattern works — what stalled is the ~6-file wiring fan-out. An orchestrator following the committed SKILL.md verbatim never reads phase-comprehension.md even with the gate enabled, and the shipped gate-check pointer at line 95 is a dead reference in production today. This is the direct preview of what 'absorbing the next 3 gates' looks like under current practice.

findings.title: Every gate added since v2.7.0 is incompletely integrated; the per-gate wiring checklist exists only implicitly
findings.evidence: rubrics/evidence-package.md checks only gate-1-scorecard.yml, gate-2-quality.yml, gate-3-review.yml (lines 9-30, summary template lines 80-86) — `grep -n '2a|2b|eval'` finds zero opt-in-gate checks, so Gate 4 passes with missing/failed 2a/2b evidence when those gates are enabled. skills/sdlc-status/SKILL.md has zero mentions of eval/2a/2b (grep exit 1) despite gate-check and sdlc-run treating them as first-class. sdlc-run/SKILL.md:184-186 lists only two Guided checkpoints (Phase 2 plan approval, Phase 5 merge approval) but phase-eval-authoring.md:14 and phase-eval-quality.md:17 define additional guided pauses with unspecified headless behavior. tests/ contains a structural test for exactly one gate (test-eval-intent-structure.sh).
findings.detail: Adding a gate touches ~10-12 files; completion has been decreasing per gate (2b skipped status+Gate-4; 2a also skipped the checkpoint list; 2c skipped everything). The Gate-4 bypass is the most consequential: it silently undermines the pipeline's own 'no unverified evidence' thesis. The fix pattern already exists in-repo — test-eval-intent-structure.sh's 30 assertions are precisely a hand-rolled linker for one gate's wiring; nothing equivalent guards the other five.

findings.title: Close/delivery flow maintained in two diverging copies (router inline vs phase-evidence.md)
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc/SKILL.md:100-209 (/sdlc close) and skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-evidence.md both contain the full merge-vs-pr strategy: identical `which gh` verification, `git push -u origin {branch}`, identical PR-body composition including the footer '🔬 Quality pipeline: Speculator | Spec: {spec_id} | Score: {overall_score}', and the compaction sequence. Step ordering already differs: close does Gate 4 -> release lock (step 2) -> close beads (step 3); phase-evidence does gate-check (step 4) -> close beads (step 5) -> remove lock (step 6) -> commit (step 7). Routing table at sdlc/SKILL.md:41 claims close merely 'invoke[s] gate-check skill with gate=evidence-package'.
findings.detail: Two sources of truth for the most consequential flow in the pipeline (the one that merges to main). They have already drifted in lock-release/beads-close ordering and will continue to drift independently — any PR-body or compaction change needs two edits with no signal when one is missed. The compaction sequence additionally appears in spec-compact/SKILL.md (both modes), making 4 total copies.

findings.title: Rubric-to-agent weight duplication in 2 of 4 scored gates; two clean counter-examples prove the better pattern
findings.evidence: agents/eval-intent-scorer/AGENT.md:78 hardcodes 'intent_coverage×0.30 + anti_pattern_detection×0.25 + journey_completeness×0.25 + implementation_independence×0.20' even though Step 1.2 (line 37) already reads rubrics/eval-intent.md (whose weight table is at lines 157-164, currently in sync). agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md hardcodes the full weight table (lines 138-141) and formula '0.30·AC + 0.30·Accuracy + 0.25·SpecFidelity + 0.15·Scope' (line 158) while also reading rubric_path (line 126). By contrast agents/spec-scorer/AGENT.md (steps 2-6) and agents/eval-quality-scorer/AGENT.md (step 7: 'Calculate the weighted overall score') defer entirely to rubric + config.
findings.detail: A rubric weight change silently desyncs the agent for the two hardcoding gates — there is no consumer-side check and the LLM will plausibly follow whichever copy it read last. Verified line-by-line on all three requested pairs: spec-quality/spec-scorer (clean), eval-intent pair (duplicated, in sync today), review/code-reviewer (see separate schema finding). The fix is a one-line edit per agent: replace the formula with 'use the weights from the rubric/config', matching spec-scorer.

findings.title: Gate 3 evidence schema defined three different ways across rubric, agent, and gate-check
findings.evidence: rubrics/review.md:137-148: `checks:` with 6 items, values `pass | fail`, fields observations/blocking_issues, and reviewer guidance 'Use a name, not a role' (line 152). agents/code-reviewer/AGENT.md:157-179: `checklist:` with per-item `verdict: pass | warn | fail` plus notes, fields blocking_issues/advisory_notes, and hardcoded `reviewer: agent` / `review_method: agent-assisted` (lines 153-154) — directly contradicting the rubric. skills/gate-check/SKILL.md:122-129: `checks:` pass|fail plus a 7th `skill_description: pass | fail | skipped` check (lines 105-115) that exists in neither the rubric nor the agent.
findings.detail: The shape of gate-3-review.yml depends on which path produced it (agent dispatch vs interactive gate-check), and the `warn` tier exists only in the agent. Gate 4 reads this file, so schema variance propagates into the evidence package. One canonical schema should live in review.md, with the agent and gate-check referencing it; skill_description should either be added to the rubric/agent as a real check or stay documented as gate-check-only.

findings.title: Duplicated executable prose has already produced a divergent-copy bug: broken lock heredoc in lib, correct copy in spec-create
findings.evidence: lib/spec-resolution.md:105 uses a quoted heredoc — `cat > docs/specs/{spec-name}/.active << 'EOF'` — so `$(echo $CLAUDE_SESSION_ID...)`, `$(basename "$(pwd)")`, and `$(date -u ...)` never expand; the lock file would contain literal command text. skills/spec-create/SKILL.md:95 has the same snippet with the correct unquoted `<< EOF`. Related CLI drift: 6 files hardcode the `beads` binary (spec-create:105, sdlc/SKILL.md:71/75/118/120-121, phase-planning.md:8/13, phase-scoring.md:47, phase-evidence.md:43-45) while sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:129 accepts `which bd || which beads` — on a bd-only host doctor passes but every scripted command fails. Also phase-scoring.md:45-49 says create an issue 'tagged spec-rework' but the shown beads command sets no tag.
findings.detail: This is the signature failure mode of prompt-as-code: bash embedded in prose, copy-pasted across files, with no shellcheck or execution test. The same class covers the spec_dir config being half-honored — `docs/specs` is hardcoded ~20 times across lib/spec-resolution.md (lines 21,56,65,73,104-121), sdlc-doctor (59,72,100), sdlc-status (30), sdlc-run (44,55), and eval-authoring (71,79,165), and the `evidence_dir` config key has no consumer at all. Extracting the 3-4 real shell snippets into lib/ scripts that skills invoke would eliminate the bug class and give spec_dir one parameterization point.

findings.title: Orchestrator self-description is stale: 'all five phases' vs seven defined phases; minor internal inconsistencies
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:14 and :193 say 'all five phases' / 'All five phases execute sequentially' while the file defines phases 1, 2, 2a, 3, 3a, 4, 5 (lines 89-164); the frontmatter description repeats the five-phase chain. Position-table rows 2a/3a (lines 72,74) use shorthand 'eval-intent.enabled: true' vs the full 'gates.eval-intent.enabled' path used everywhere else. Phase 3a (lines 152-156) is the only phase using absolute '${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/skills/sdlc-run/references/...' prose instead of the '→ Read references/phase-*.md' arrow format — evidence of unnormalized later patching.
findings.detail: Low individually, but in a prompt-as-code system the prose IS the executable, and an LLM orchestrator reading 'all five phases' may legitimately skip opt-in phases. Same class: CHANGELOG has a 2.6.0 entry (line 36) for a version plugin.json never carried, omits 6 shipped versions, and 2.x entries are undated.

findings.title: Context budget is well-managed; the one violation is the router's 160 inline lines for implement/close
findings.evidence: Always-loaded skill frontmatter totals 4,287 bytes across 9 skills; agent frontmatter 368-503 bytes each. Per-phase incremental load via references is 24-164 lines; worst-case full orchestration read (router + sdlc-run + lib + all 8 refs) is 48,563 bytes (~12k tokens). skills/sdlc/SKILL.md is 230 lines, of which lines 47-209 are the inline /sdlc implement and /sdlc close implementations loaded on every /sdlc invocation including ones that need neither.
findings.detail: The progressive-disclosure design (high-level model inline, mechanics in references) is the right call and should be the template for the router too: extracting implement/close into sub-skills (or lib references) shrinks the router to pure routing (~70 lines), fixes the misleading routing-table entry for close, and removes one of the two duplicate close-flow copies in the same stroke.

top_recommendations: Build the missing 'compiler': template tests/test-eval-intent-structure.sh into a per-gate structural test (rubric exists, agent exists, phase reference exists, position-table row present, gate-check section present, doctor template block present, sdlc-status row present, evidence-package check present, weights consistent between rubric and agent) plus a release-consistency check (plugin.json == CHANGELOG top == marketplace.json, JSON parses), and run them in a minimal CI workflow. This single mechanism would have caught the phantom 2.8.0 bump, the Gate 2c orphaning, the 2a/2b Gate-4 bypass, and the Intent Alignment/Spec Fidelity rename drift — it is the prerequisite for absorbing 3 more gates.

Single-source the gate registry: a small lib/gates.md table (gate id, evidence filename, config key, default threshold, phase position, opt-in flag) that sdlc-run position detection, gate-check, sdlc-status, the doctor template, and evidence-package.md all reference instead of each re-encoding the gate list. Today the gate list is duplicated in at least 5 places and every new gate must update all of them by hand; this is the structural change that makes the next 3 gates additive rather than multiplicative.

Deduplicate the close/delivery flow: extract /sdlc implement and /sdlc close from the router into dedicated skills (or have close delegate to references/phase-evidence.md), reconcile the lock-release-vs-beads-close ordering difference between the two existing copies, and fix the routing-table line 41 misdescription. The router becomes pure routing (~70 lines), and the highest-stakes flow (merge to main) gets one source of truth.

Cut a 2.8.1 release through a written RELEASE.md checklist and start tagging: the installed plugin currently self-reports 2.7.0 and emits an outdated doctor config template; the marketplace clone is a commit behind. Add the version-consistency script (rec 1) as a mandatory pre-release step so the three-step manual dance is verified rather than trusted.

Wire or shelve Gate 2c, and while touching the scorer agents remove the hardcoded weight formulas from eval-intent-scorer (AGENT.md:78) and comprehension-scorer (AGENT.md:138-141,158) so all four scored gates defer to rubric+config like spec-scorer does; reconcile the three-way Gate 3 evidence schema (pick review.md as canonical, decide warn-tier and skill_description placement). Also extract the duplicated lock/worktree-scan bash into lib/ scripts — fixing the quoted-heredoc bug class and giving spec_dir its single parameterization point.

critiques.assessment:
Speculator's measurement architecture is unusually thoughtful on paper — concrete per-band calibration anchors in every scored rubric, anti-inflation guidance, per-dimension minimums, blocking flags, and a deliberate decomposition where each gate targets a distinct artifact (spec → prose evals → test execution → test quality → code → evidence package). The rubric anchors in spec-quality.md and eval-quality.md are genuinely good LLM-judge prompts: each band has a realistic code/spec snippet, and the intent-vs-letter framing (Intent Verifiability, Intent Fidelity, the unshipped Spec Fidelity) shows the authors understand Goodhart risks better than most. But the system's actual measurement validity is unproven and, where evidence exists, undermined: the headline 18-point benchmark measures the judge rewarding compliance with its own feedback (spec scores only, manual methodology, no preserved artifacts, unpinned scorer model, simplified non-production prompt), and the single end-to-end harness run contradicts the core thesis — a spec improved from 7.0 to 8.3 produced byte-identical functional results (12/20 vs 12/20). The calibration protocol that would convert "LLM-as-judge" from assumed-valid to measured exists in code (calibration.py) and has never been run once.

The two deepest validity problems are (1) threshold resolution far below noise, and (2) self-reported, unverified evidence. The trust ladder makes its most consequential decision — Full Auto vs Guided vs self-improvement — on 0.2-point distinctions (7.8/8.0), while the repo's own benchmark measured round-to-round sigma of 0.32–0.46 on raw scores for the same model and PRD; with six integer dimensions, a single 7-vs-8 judgment call on one dimension moves the overall by 0.10–0.20 and flips the autonomy mode. There is no repeat-scoring, no variance estimation, and the scorer reads the pass threshold before scoring (a known score-attraction bias). Meanwhile, every gate's evidence YAML is written by an LLM in the same pipeline session with zero independent verification — no script reruns tests, validates schemas, or checks the weighted-average arithmetic, and Gate 4 only checks that files exist with result: pass. The dogfood evidence shows this isn't theoretical: SPEC-001 passed Gate 2 on 25 pre-existing secrets-scan tests unrelated to the feature (coverage "skipped", zero feature tests), and the Gate 3 reviewer documented a direct quantitative spec violation (15-20 calibration examples required, ~4 delivered) and passed spec_alignment anyway with a "functional goal is served" rationalization — the letter-vs-spirit failure mode occurring inside the gate designed to catch it.

The honest summary: this is a well-designed instrument that has never been pointed at ground truth. The gates likely do add value as structured forcing functions (the self-improvement loop demonstrably makes specs longer and more detailed), but the system currently cannot distinguish "specs got better" from "specs got better at pleasing the judge," cannot distinguish 8.3 from 7.9, and cannot detect an agent that fabricates or vacuously satisfies evidence. The fixes are cheap relative to what's built: one real outcome experiment with a no-feedback control arm, one deterministic evidence-verification script, one calibration run, and threshold bands sized to measured variance.

findings.title: Zero valid evidence that gate scores predict implementation quality; the only end-to-end datapoint contradicts the thesis
findings.evidence: benchmarks/results/3-round-spec-quality.yml line 4: 'Methodology: Manual 3-round benchmark' — 18 points are spec scores only, no implementations, no preserved specs/scorecards. benchmarks/runs/bench-2026-03-28-001/: spec improved 7.0→8.3 (iteration-log.yml) but functional-results.yml is byte-identical for original and improved (verified via diff: 'IDENTICAL', 12/20 both), with misaligned error strings (F03 'Save multiple named routes' → 'No Feels like text found'); improved impl has no judge-scorecard.yml. calibration.py implemented, zero calibration artifacts anywhere (find returned only source/tests).
findings.detail: The system's headline claims (F4 'Speculator ALWAYS improves specs 9/9', F6 '$1 model + Speculator matches $3 model') measure judge-score lift, which is the metric, not the outcome. build_feedback_prompt (scoring.py:246-268) feeds the scorer's own flags back as improvement instructions, then the same scorer re-scores — a closed Goodhart loop with no control arm (e.g., regenerate without feedback) to separate feedback value from extra-pass compute. F5's 'all models converge to 8.4-8.6' is more parsimoniously explained as the judge's preferred score range (ceiling effect) than real quality convergence. The thesis 'spec quality predicts implementation quality' currently has zero valid supporting data points and one (broken-runner-tainted) contradicting one.

findings.title: Trust-ladder thresholds resolve differences smaller than measured score noise — 8.3 vs 7.9 is plausibly noise
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:95-112: Full Auto at >=7.8, self-improvement trigger 8.0, guided 7.0 — a 0.2-wide decision band. rubrics/spec-quality.md:277-288: six integer dimensions weighted 0.20/0.20/0.20/0.15/0.15/0.10, so one dimension moving 7→8 shifts overall by 0.10-0.20.
