The repo's own benchmark aggregates (results/3-round-spec-quality.yml:72-90) show sigma_raw 0.32-0.46 across rounds for identical model+PRD. Grep across skills/, agents/, rubrics/ finds zero variance/repeat-scoring machinery. agents/spec-scorer/AGENT.md process step 4: 'Read the project config for threshold and scoring weights' — the judge knows the pass bar before scoring.
findings.detail: The most consequential decision the system makes (autonomous execution vs human oversight) hinges on whether a single-dispatch LLM judge says 7.9 or 8.0 — within measured noise even before isolating judge variance from generation variance. The intent_verifiability_min hard gate (>=8) is a single integer judgment call (8 vs 7, a notoriously unreliable LLM-judge distinction) that alone flips Full Auto to Guided. The scorer reading the threshold before scoring invites score-attraction bias — the dogfooded SPEC-001 landed at exactly 8.0 (docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-1-scorecard.yml), precisely the boundary that skips self-improvement. No test-retest data exists to size the bands.

findings.title: All gate evidence is self-reported by the pipeline's own LLM with no independent verification — fabrication or vacuous satisfaction is undetectable
findings.evidence: skills/gate-check/SKILL.md and all skills are pure prose (no executable code in the plugin). rubrics/evidence-package.md: Gate 4 checks only that gate-1/2/3 YAML files exist with result: pass. hooks/hooks.json: single warn-only prompt hook on git commit checking Gate 1 scorecard existence ('Do NOT block the commit'). Dogfood proof: docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-2-quality.yml shows tests_pass: true, test_count: 25 — 'All 25 secrets-scan tests pass (only test suite in project)' — the feature (7 markdown deliverables) shipped with zero feature tests; coverage_threshold: skipped; ac_traceability: false in .claude/sdlc.local.md.
findings.detail: Gate 2's letter ('a test run was executed and all tests passed', rubrics/code-quality.md:33-38) was satisfied in the project's own flagship run by an unrelated pre-existing suite. Nothing re-executes commands, validates evidence schema, or checks that the recorded overall equals the weighted sum of dimensions (LLM arithmetic is itself a reliability risk). An agent in a hurry can write gate-2-quality.yml claiming tests pass without running anything; no layer of the system — gate-check, Gate 4, or the warn-only hook — would catch it. Gate 4 also never checks gate-2a/2b evidence even when those gates are enabled (evidence-package.md verifies only gates 1/2/3), so opt-in gates can be silently skipped at close.

findings.title: Same-model self-grading at every gate; the cold-read/independent-judge principle exists only in the unshipped Gate 2c
findings.evidence: agents/spec-scorer/AGENT.md:12, agents/eval-intent-scorer, agents/eval-quality-scorer/AGENT.md:14, agents/code-reviewer/AGENT.md:13 — all 'model: sonnet', the same model family that writes the specs, evals, tests, and code in the pipeline. The cold-read constraint ('implementer is never its own judge') appears only in the untracked skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md:71-80 and agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md, which nothing executable dispatches (sdlc-run/SKILL.md phase table lines 68-77 has no comprehension row). Self-fix cycles (references/phase-eval-quality.md step 2.4, phase-review.md step 2) have the judged party revise the artifact specifically to address the judge's flags, then the same judge re-scores.
findings.detail: Same-family judge bias is unmeasured (calibration never run) and structurally unmitigated in everything that ships. The self-fix pattern compounds it: 'pass after self-fix' measures compliance with the judge's specific feedback, not independent quality — the production analogue of the benchmark's Goodhart loop. The benchmark additionally never pinned the scorer model (scoring.py:196-201 runs claude -p with no --model flag), so even the recorded 'scorer_model: claude-sonnet-4-6' is operator-environment-dependent.

findings.title: Concrete judge-leniency failure in the dogfood evidence: Gate 3 passed spec_alignment despite documenting a direct quantitative constraint violation
findings.evidence: docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-3-review.yml: 'The spec says 15-20 [calibration examples] per dimension; this implementation has 4 scored bands with 1-2 examples each — materially fewer than 15-20. This is an advisory gap, not a blocking issue... the functional goal (LLM judge reliability) is served.' checks.spec_alignment: pass; result: pass.
findings.detail: This is the canonical letter-vs-spirit failure occurring inside the gate whose job is spec alignment: the reviewer found, quantified, and then rationalized away a violated spec constraint — using exactly the 'intent is served anyway' reasoning the MANIFESTO warns about. It also directly degrades measurement validity downstream: the constraint it waived (15-20 calibration examples) is the rubric's own stated requirement for reliable judging, so the eval-quality judge now operates with ~4 anchors per dimension while its rubric self-declares that insufficient. One real pipeline run, one observed leniency rationalization — the base rate is unknown but nonzero.

findings.title: Gate 2a rubric wording rewards unfalsifiable evals and is conditioned on the spec's self-declared anti-patterns — both gameable
findings.evidence: rubrics/eval-intent.md:124-151: Implementation Independence scores 1-3 for evals that 'name specific files, functions, config keys' (blocking flag at line 180) while the 9-10 anchor is prose like 'a developer can answer the three key questions... regardless of whether the output is YAML, JSON, plain text'. rubrics/eval-intent.md:181: anti-pattern blocking flag applies only 'when anti-pattern section is non-empty'. Anti-Pattern Detection 7-8 band requires covering only 'at least half of the spec's named anti-patterns' (line 52).
findings.detail: An agent in a hurry games Gate 2a two ways: (1) strip all concrete artifacts from evals — vague, observer-relative prose ('a developer can understand X') scores highest on Implementation Independence and Intent Coverage but is unverifiable, so the gate optimizes against the testability that Gate 1 rewards; (2) author specs with empty/thin anti-pattern sections, which makes Gate 2a strictly easier (the blocking flag deactivates) at the cost of only part of a 0.15-weight Gate 1 dimension. Similarly, the universal 'always include at least one flag' anti-gaming rule (spec-scorer AGENT.md:92 and all scorers) is trivially satisfiable with an advisory flag — only blocking flags have teeth.

findings.title: No fast deterministic feedback layer — every check, including arithmetic and mechanical completeness, is a slow LLM pass
findings.evidence: All 9 skills are prose-only (no scripts in skills/ or lib/). Gate 2's underlying commands (pytest/lint/tsc, rubrics/code-quality.md) are deterministic but invoked and transcribed by an LLM; lint/build/type-check default false, coverage skippable, ac_traceability default false (.claude/sdlc.local.md, sdlc-doctor template). Gate 4's 'mechanical' completeness check is LLM-executed prose (evidence-package.md). hooks/hooks.json is a prompt hook (30s timeout, warn-only). The only fast tests in the repo (tests/test-eval-intent-structure.sh 30 checks, test-secrets-scan.sh 25 checks) validate the plugin's own files, not user pipelines.
findings.detail: A full pipeline run requires ~4-6 LLM scorer dispatches before merge, each minutes-slow and non-deterministic, while cheap deterministic checks that would catch real failure modes are absent: evidence YAML schema validation, weighted-average arithmetic verification, threshold comparison, weights-sum-to-1.0, test-command re-execution, and 'did the diff add any test files' (which would have caught the SPEC-001 vacuous Gate 2 pass). The system delegates exactly the checks LLMs are worst at (arithmetic, exhaustive file checks) to the LLM and reserves no role for scripts.

findings.title: Benchmark scorer prompt is not the production rubric — Spec-Bench results don't measure the shipped gate even on its own terms
findings.evidence: benchmarks/src/spec_bench/scoring.py:56-106 SCORING_PROMPT_TEMPLATE: generic 4-line band anchors, no per-dimension calibration examples, no anti-inflation guidance ('most first drafts should score 5-7'), no per-dimension minimum, no blocking-flag-forces-fail semantics. Production rubric rubrics/spec-quality.md has all of these (lines 5-7, 20-54 per-band snippets, 290-294, 300-311). scoring.py:196-201: no --model flag on claude -p.
findings.detail: Even setting aside the manual-methodology problem with the 18-point result, the harness's score_spec measures a different instrument than the one /sdlc score deploys: the inlined prompt drops the exact features (anchors, anti-inflation, minimums) that constitute the production judge's calibration. Benchmark lift/convergence numbers therefore cannot validate or tune the production gate. The improvement prompt (IMPROVEMENT_PROMPT_TEMPLATE, scoring.py:108-133) also discloses the pass threshold to the spec improver, further entangling the measurement with the target.

findings.title: Gate overlap: three gates measure AC-to-test mapping with three different scorers, while no gate measures runtime behavior
findings.evidence: AC mapping is checked by Gate 2 ac_traceability (rubrics/code-quality.md:7-29), Gate 2b ac_coverage at weight 0.25 (rubrics/eval-quality.md:13-69), and Gate 3 correctness/'Do all acceptance criteria have corresponding implementation' plus spec_alignment (agents/code-reviewer/AGent.md:90-94, 137-142). No gate executes the built feature; the only behavioral signal is tests authored by the implementer itself. All LLM-scored gates share model: sonnet and similar rubric structure, so leniency biases correlate across gates rather than providing independent coverage.
findings.detail: The decomposition is conceptually disjoint by artifact (spec/evals/tests/code), which is sound, but in failure-mode space the redundancy is concentrated: AC-mapping is triple-checked while 'the feature actually works when run' is checked zero times (Spec-Bench's Playwright layer exists only in the benchmark harness, never in the pipeline). Defense-in-depth against a lenient judge requires decorrelated judges; here a single systematic bias (e.g., the rationalization observed in gate-3-review.yml) passes through every layer. Gate 2b — the layer specifically designed to catch nominal AC mappings ('test name references the AC but assertions don't verify it', eval-quality.md:18) — is opt-in and disabled everywhere including Speculator's own config (.claude/sdlc.local.md omits eval-quality).

findings.title: Near-zero weights make two Gate 2b dimensions decorative, and the deterministic dimensions are the de-weighted ones
findings.evidence: rubrics/eval-quality.md:436-447: Assertion Density 0.03, Test Independence 0.02. eval-quality-scorer AGENT.md:54: 'For Dimensions 6, 7 (Deterministic): Count assertions per test... verify mechanically.'
findings.detail: The two dimensions the rubric says can be verified mechanically — the most reliable measurements in the whole gate — contribute 5% combined to the overall and matter only via the per-dimension minimum of 4 (i.e., only catastrophic failures bite). Meanwhile 95% of the score rests on LLM judgment. Whether this is intended tripwire design is undocumented; as measurement design it inverts the reliability-weighting you'd want.

findings.title: Override-pass and skeleton-spec paths create low-friction routes through gates, mitigated but not audited
findings.evidence: skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md:79: position detection accepts 'result: pass OR override-pass' as Gate 2a satisfied. skills/eval-authoring/SKILL.md:140-151: override requires non-empty justification, 'does not affect trust score'. SKILL.md:35-45: quoted-string entry generates a TODO-skeleton spec that the self-improvement loop fleshes out from context, then scores — the same agent authors, improves, and is judged on the spec.
findings.detail: Overrides require human interaction (reasonable), but justification text is never evaluated and nothing aggregates override frequency, so habitual overriding would be invisible. The skeleton path means a one-line user request can become a fully agent-authored spec that the agent's same-family judge then scores for autonomy level — the human's intent enters the system as a single sentence, yet intent-fidelity gates downstream treat the generated spec as ground truth.

findings.title: Gate 3 evidence schema drift means review evidence shape depends on which path produced it
findings.evidence: rubrics/review.md specifies 6 checks pass|fail; agents/code-reviewer/AGENT.md:157-179 outputs checklist with pass|warn|fail verdicts + notes; the actual dogfood artifact docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-3-review.yml uses flat 'checks:' with 7 entries including skill_description (which exists in neither rubric nor agent) and reviewer: 'Claude Sonnet 4.6 (agent-assisted)' contradicting AGENT.md's hardcoded reviewer: agent.
findings.detail: For a measurement system, inconsistent evidence schemas undermine the one mechanical check that exists (Gate 4 reads these files) and make longitudinal analysis of gate outcomes (e.g., warn rates, which checks fail most) impossible. The 'warn' tier exists in the agent but has no defined semantics in the rubric and no aggregation anywhere.

top_recommendations: Run one real outcome experiment before any further gate-building: execute the 8-target matrix end-to-end through the fixed Option C harness with a no-feedback control arm (regenerate spec without scorer flags), pin the scorer model (--model in scoring.py:196), and use the production rubric text as the scoring prompt. This is the only way to convert 'spec score predicts implementation quality' from thesis to measurement — today it has zero valid datapoints and one contradicting one.

Add a thin deterministic verification layer (a single scripts/verify-evidence.sh): validate evidence YAML schema, recompute weighted overall from dimension scores, check threshold/minimum comparisons, verify weights sum to 1.0, re-run the recorded test command and compare counts, and require that the diff adds test files when Gate 2 claims feature tests. This closes the self-reported-evidence hole (the SPEC-001 vacuous Gate 2 pass), gives the pipeline its only fast feedback, and catches LLM arithmetic errors — all without any LLM call.

Size trust-ladder bands to measured noise: score each spec N=3 times (or at minimum measure test-retest sigma on a fixed spec corpus once), use the median, and require the score to clear full_auto_threshold by more than the measured sigma before granting Full Auto. Simultaneously blind the scorer to the pass threshold (have the orchestrator, not the scorer agent, apply threshold/minimum logic) to remove score-attraction bias at the 7.8/8.0 boundary.

Run the calibration protocol that already exists (spec-bench calibrate, 1.0-pt divergence tolerance) on the dogfood spec and any new pipeline runs, and ship Gate 2c's cold-read/independent-judge pattern to the gates that exist: dispatch scorers as fresh subagents that never see the implementer's reasoning, and consider a different judge model for Gate 3 to decorrelate failure modes. Track override-pass frequency and self-fix pass rates as judge-health metrics.

Close the specific gameability loopholes in rubric wording: make Gate 2 require feature-mapped tests (ac_traceability default true, or a 'new tests added' check) and an explicit N/A-rationale for prompt-only changesets; make Gate 2a's anti-pattern blocking flag fire when the spec's anti-pattern section is empty for medium+ risk specs (instead of deactivating); and add to Gate 3 an explicit rule that documented quantitative spec-constraint violations cannot be downgraded below 'recommended' without human sign-off — directly addressing the rationalization observed in gate-3-review.yml.

critiques.assessment:
Speculator's strategic position in mid-2026 is "right thesis, unproven product, stalled execution." The MANIFESTO's core claim — nobody quantitatively scores spec quality — was defensible in its March 2026 survey, but the moat it describes is thin: a rubric + LLM-as-judge is trivially replicable by Kiro, spec-kit, or Claude Code's native plan/review modes the moment any of them decides to add a score. The durable moat would be *validated calibration data* — proof that gate scores predict implementation outcomes — and that is precisely what doesn't exist. I verified this directly: the headline 18-point result is self-described as a "Manual 3-round benchmark" measuring only judge scores in a self-referential feedback loop, and the single end-to-end harness run ever recorded shows a spec improved from 7.0 to 8.3 producing byte-identical functional results (MD5-confirmed) with no judge scorecard for the improved build. The project's one real data point is null-to-negative on its own thesis. Notably, Speculator's docs engage with Amazon Kiro only as a disaster anecdote (the NBJ dark-code narrative) rather than as the competing spec-driven product it now is — the landscape model is filtered through one newsletter's framing and is ~3 months stale in a fast-moving space.

The genuine differentiators rank differently than the ROADMAP invests in them. (1) SYSTEM-SPEC compaction / "specs as living organisms" is the most defensible long-term idea — per-feature specs are commoditized, but a cumulative, provenance-tracked institutional memory that agents reason over is something neither Kiro nor spec-kit does — yet it's the least developed: SYSTEM-SPEC.md is 35 lines with one spec, the flagship v2.8.0 feature was compacted in the parent workspace instead, and the repo's own SYSTEM-SPEC sits at a path the skills can't even find. (2) The trust ladder (score-gated autonomy with self-improvement trigger deliberately above the full-auto threshold) is genuinely thoughtful design that native plan modes don't have — but its value is entirely downstream of judges nobody has validated. (3) Eval-first Gate 2a is shipped and real. (4) The comprehension gate would be novel, but it's the most expensive gate, default-off, with calibration ("the build," per ROADMAP's own words) untouched — and the project's track record on calibration work is zero-for-two (calibration protocol never executed once; benchmark dataset still one PRD).

On the in-flight bet: Gate 2c as currently scoped is the wrong next move, and the ROADMAP itself says so without noticing — it labels mutation-testing validation of Gate 2b "the single highest-leverage research move because it gives us the empirical answer to 'does this actually work?'" (ROADMAP.md:128) and then ranks it *behind* building a third unvalidated judge. For a solo maintainer with a 5.5-week-dormant repo, 948 lines of orchestration prose already exhibiting documented drift (stale "five phases," duplicated close logic, bd/beads CLI mismatch, Gate 4 blind to opt-in gate evidence), adding gate #7 compounds maintenance debt against an unproven foundation. The pragmatic resolution: rescue the stranded work to a branch immediately (it's the only copy, 45 days unbacked), ship the *wiring* minimally bound to risk_level:high as experimental (ROADMAP's own v3 suggestion, pulled forward), and redirect the calibration effort into validation of the gates that already exist. Cut the public leaderboard, defer spec-drift detection until SYSTEM-SPEC has a corpus to drift against, and retire the unreproducible 18-point result and the unmeasured "$1 model matches $3 model" cost claim from marketing before someone in a now-crowded space audits them.

findings.title: Core thesis has zero valid supporting evidence; the only end-to-end data point contradicts it
findings.evidence: benchmarks/runs/3-round-spec-quality/results.yml:4 ('Methodology: Manual 3-round benchmark' — judge scores only, no implementations, specs/scorecards not preserved); benchmarks/runs/bench-2026-03-28-001/implementations/: functional-results.yml MD5-identical (a2521944c53d47f2f0985d24486ccc41) for cc-vanilla-sonnet-original and -improved despite spec 7.0→8.3 (iteration-log.yml); improved dir has no judge-scorecard.yml; scoring.py:196-201 runs claude -p with no --model pin; scoring.py:246 feedback loop is self-referential (same scorer judges compliance with its own flags)
findings.detail: Speculator's entire value proposition is 'spec score predicts implementation quality.' The marketing-grade 18-point result measures only how a judge scores specs revised to that judge's own feedback (Goodhart loop, no control arm, unreproducible — raw artifacts not preserved). The single harness-produced outcome data point shows an improved spec yielding identical functional results. In a 2026 landscape where Kiro/spec-kit/native plan modes commoditize the workflow, validated predictive power is the only defensible moat — and it is empty. This converts every downstream design decision (trust ladder thresholds, gate weights, self-improvement loop) into untested assumptions.

findings.title: The #1 roadmap priority is stranded half-built and uncommitted for 45 days, with ROADMAP now wrong in both directions
findings.evidence: ROADMAP.md:23,45 ('Designed, not yet built — top priority'); git status: agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md (290 lines) and skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md (164 lines) untracked, rubrics/comprehension.md modified (+55/−10), all dated 2026-04-28; grep -rn 'comprehension' across skills/sdlc/SKILL.md, skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md, skills/gate-check/SKILL.md returns zero hits; sole name-drop at gate-check/SKILL.md:95; last commit a769bf2 2026-05-04
findings.detail: ~870 lines of Gate 2c design work exist only in the working tree — no branch, no remote, no tracking issue (the repo's beads DB is empty post-Dolt-migration). The orchestrator would never execute it even with gates.comprehension.enabled:true because no skill references phase-comprehension.md. ROADMAP says 'not yet built' (understates), while the working tree implies active development (overstates — dormant 5.5 weeks). This is the project's stated top priority sitting in the single most fragile possible state: one `git clean -fd` from deletion.

findings.title: Roadmap priority inversion: validation is self-identified as highest-leverage yet ranked behind building another unvalidated judge
findings.evidence: ROADMAP.md:128: mutation-testing validation is 'the single highest-leverage research move because it gives us the empirical answer to "does this actually work?"' — yet it is item #2 'after Gate 2c' (ROADMAP.md:114); ROADMAP.md:104: 'Calibration is the build... Without a strong calibration set, the Accuracy dimension drifts and the gate becomes performative'; calibration.py exists but zero calibration artifacts in any run dir; benchmarks/prds/ contains one PRD
findings.detail: The project's own documents contain the correct strategic analysis and then don't follow it. Gate 2c's critical path is a 40-60-example calibration corpus — exactly the dataset-curation work the project has failed to do twice already (calibration protocol never run once; Spec-Bench dataset stuck at 1 PRD since March). Building a third LLM-judge gate before validating the first two compounds an unproven stack. The right sequencing for a solo maintainer is: validate Gates 1/2b empirically (mutation correlation or one real end-to-end matrix run), then decide whether a comprehension gate's expense is justified by what validation reveals.

findings.title: The most defensible differentiator (living-spec compaction) is the least invested-in, and its dogfooding is broken
findings.evidence: SYSTEM-SPEC.md is 35 lines with one compacted spec (SPEC-001); v2.8.0's flagship Gate 2a (SPEC-040) was dogfooded in the parent claudeclaw workspace so the repo's living spec has no record of its biggest feature; SYSTEM-SPEC.md sits at repo root while skills resolve {spec_dir}/SYSTEM-SPEC.md (spec-create/SKILL.md:33, spec-compact/SKILL.md:43, sdlc/SKILL.md:143) — docs/specs/SYSTEM-SPEC.md does not exist, so impact-awareness and compat checks silently no-op in Speculator's own repo
findings.detail: Per-feature spec scoring is replicable; a cumulative, provenance-trailed SYSTEM-SPEC that future agents reason over (MANIFESTO VI 'Specs as Living Organisms') is the idea competitors don't have and the natural foundation for the deferred spec-drift work. But it has one fold-in, a path bug that disables it in its own repo, and the downstream SPEC-042 domain-split work was done in claudeclaw with the Speculator-side compactor updates deferred (claw-1ff1, which exists in no tracker here). Strategy should rebalance toward exercising this differentiator: fix the path, run the next 3-5 specs in-repo, accumulate real multi-spec provenance.

findings.title: Release and trust infrastructure contradicts the product's own pitch
findings.evidence: Installed cache ~/.claude/plugins/cache/dmokong-plugins/speculator/2.8.0 carries plugin.json declaring 2.7.0 (bump commit 6703adc never edited plugin.json; real bump f72bb03 never re-released); zero git tags across 171 commits; no .github/workflows (ROADMAP.md:192 acknowledges); CHANGELOG missing entries for 2.0.0 rebrand, 1.2.0, 2.2.0-2.3.1 and documents a 2.6.0 the manifest never carried; installed doctor template emits outdated 4-dimension config schema
findings.detail: A plugin whose entire identity is 'evidence, gates, and quality enforcement' ships with a version lie in its installed artifact, no CI verifying its own test claims, and a release process living only in the parent workspace's memory file. This is a credibility risk if the project seeks adoption in a crowded space, and a practical one: users doctored via the installed plugin get a stale config schema. The fix is cheap (2.8.1 re-release + a shell-script version-consistency test + tags) and proportionally high-leverage.

findings.title: Prompt-surface sprawl is compounding faster than a solo maintainer can keep consistent — argues for consolidation before any new gate
findings.evidence: 948 lines of orchestration prose (sdlc-run/SKILL.md 230 + 8 references 488 + router 230); sdlc-run/SKILL.md:14 still claims 'five phases' while defining seven; /sdlc close duplicated between sdlc/SKILL.md:100-209 and references/phase-evidence.md with divergent step ordering; skills hardcode 'beads' CLI while doctor accepts bd (sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:129) — commands fail on bd-only hosts; rubrics/evidence-package.md checks only gates 1/2/3, so enabled opt-in gates (2a/2b, future 2c) can be bypassed at Gate 4; sdlc-status blind to gates 2a/2b; three-way Gate 3 evidence-schema drift (review.md vs code-reviewer/AGENT.md vs gate-check/SKILL.md)
findings.detail: Every gate added multiplies the n-way consistency surface (rubric, agent, gate-check branch, sdlc-run phase row, position-detection row, doctor template, status display, Gate 4 check, README counts). The existing six gates already show eight+ documented drift instances, including one functional bypass (Gate 4 ignoring opt-in evidence). Gate 2c adds ~870 more lines to this surface. Strategically this is the strongest argument for a consolidation release (dedupe close logic, single-source weights, extend Gate 4, structural tests per gate cloned from test-eval-intent-structure.sh) before any expansion.

findings.title: Positioning claims have a shelf life and are aging unmaintained
findings.evidence: MANIFESTO.md:51 ('In March 2026, we surveyed the landscape... We couldn't find one'); MANIFESTO.md:184 still lists 'Spec quality benchmark dataset' as future work contradicting README.md:585/ROADMAP item 4 framing; ROADMAP.md:16 treats Amazon Kiro solely as a failure case study via Nate B Jones's framing, never as a competing spec-driven product; repo dormant since 2026-05-04
findings.detail: The 'nobody measures spec quality' claim is a point-in-time survey now ~3 months old in a space where spec-driven development went mainstream. The docs' entire competitive lens is one newsletter author's narrative (NBJ cited 5+ times across MANIFESTO/ROADMAP). There is no mechanism or stated intent to re-survey. If a competitor ships quantitative spec scoring, Speculator's manifesto becomes falsifiable marketing. Lower severity because fixing it is editorial, but it should accompany any public push.

findings.title: Cut candidates: leaderboard, spec-drift detection, and unmeasured cost claims
findings.evidence: ROADMAP.md:143-154 (item 4: public dataset + leaderboard — 'Need: at least 5 PRDs... currently only weather-transport'); ROADMAP.md:131-141 (item 3: spec drift detection against a 35-line single-spec SYSTEM-SPEC); results.yml cost framing ('$1 model + Speculator matches $3 model') rests on list prices — all measured token fields are 0 (scoring.py iteration_tokens never incremented; adapter grep never matches claude -p output)
findings.detail: For a solo maintainer: (a) a community leaderboard with one PRD and no community is an operations sinkhole — cut until ≥5 PRDs exist organically; (b) spec-drift detection cannot ship before the living spec it diffs against has a real corpus — explicitly defer behind in-repo dogfooding; (c) the cost-efficiency claim should be retracted or re-measured since the harness's token accounting is dead code; (d) smaller cuts: the near-zero Gate 2b weights (0.03/0.02) and the duplicated /sdlc close inline flow. Saying no to these funds the validation work that actually defends the thesis.

top_recommendations: Rescue Gate 2c today, then ship it minimal rather than maximal: commit the untracked agent + phase reference + rubric edits to a feature branch immediately (only copy, 45 days unbacked), then wire just the orchestration (sdlc-run phase row, gate-check branch, doctor template) bound to risk_level:high per ROADMAP's own v3 suggestion, labeled experimental — defer the 40-60-example calibration corpus rather than blocking on it or pretending it will happen.

Invert the roadmap: run validation before building any new judge. Execute one real end-to-end Spec-Bench matrix run with the fixed Option C harness (pinned scorer model, no-feedback control arm, working token accounting), and start the Gate 2b mutation-testing correlation that ROADMAP.md:128 already calls 'the single highest-leverage research move.' The thesis currently has zero valid data points and one contradicting one — this is the existential gap.

Cut a 2.8.1 trust release: fix the installed-cache version lie (cached plugin.json says 2.7.0), ship the updated doctor template, add minimal CI (3 existing test suites + a version-consistency check across plugin.json/CHANGELOG/marketplace.json), tag releases, and fix the stale doc claims (MANIFESTO VII benchmark bullet, 'five phases', '8 rubrics', '57 tests').

Re-invest in the living-spec differentiator: fix the SYSTEM-SPEC.md path mismatch so Speculator's own pipeline stops silently skipping impact checks, run the next 3-5 specs through docs/specs/ in this repo (not the parent workspace), and close the SPEC-042 compactor follow-up — multi-spec provenance in SYSTEM-SPEC is the moat Kiro/spec-kit don't have.

Cut explicitly: the public dataset + leaderboard (until ≥5 PRDs exist), spec-drift detection (until SYSTEM-SPEC has a corpus), the unreproducible 18-point result and the unmeasured '$1 vs $3 model' cost claim from marketing, and the duplicated /sdlc close inline logic — a solo maintainer's strategy is defined by what gets refused.

verified_recommendations.rec: Fix the install path first: cut a 2.8.1 re-release (correct plugin.json version, current doctor config template), change sdlc-doctor's plugin-wiring check to verify hooks/hooks.json instead of grepping settings.local.json, and align the README --init claim — the new-user first session currently includes a version lie and a guaranteed false warning.
verified_recommendations.reasoning: Two of the three problems are real and file-verified; the third is real but mislocated, which changes what a 2.8.1 release actually accomplishes. (1) The "guaranteed false warning" is fully confirmed at HEAD: /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/skills/sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:47-53 verifies plugin wiring by grepping `.claude/settings.local.json` for "SDLC PRE-COMMIT", but that hook ships in the plugin's hooks/hooks.json and is auto-registered on plugin install — no install path ever writes it to a consumer's settings.local.json. Since skills/spec-create/SKILL.md:28 runs sdlc-doctor on every /sdlc start, every fresh project's first session emits a false "Pre-commit hook: not registered" WARN, and the --fix path (SKILL.md:180) would create a duplicate hook registration. (2) The README --init misalignment is confirmed: README.md:34 claims `--init` will "register the pre-commit hook"; the doctor's --init procedure (SKILL.md:187-281) does no hook registration. (3) The "version lie" is real but NOT in the repo and NOT in a new-user install today: both plugin.json files at HEAD say 2.8.0 and are pushed (local == origin/main at a769bf2), and HEAD's doctor --init template already includes the opt-in gate config. The lie is in the shipped artifact history: the 2.8.0 release commit (6703adc, 2026-04-16) bumped only CHANGELOG.md while .claude-plugin/plugin.json stayed 2.7.0 until f72bb03 (2026-04-28); the currently installed cache (~/.claude/plugins/cache/dmokong-plugins/speculator/2.8.0/, gitCommitSha 6703adc) contains manifest version 2.7.0 inside a directory labeled 2.8.0 and a doctor SKILL.md missing the a769bf2 template. So "correct plugin.json version, current doctor config template" are no-op repo changes — 2.8.1's real value is propagating already-fixed content through the version-keyed distribution channel (three distinct contents have shipped as "2.8.0": 6703adc, f72bb03, a769bf2; no git tags exist to disambiguate). Cost/benefit is good for a solo maintainer: ~3 small file edits plus the already-practiced three-step release (<1 hour) to remove a false warning firing in every user's first session. No conflict with stated direction: ROADMAP.md's priority (Gate 2c) is orthogonal, and MANIFESTO.md's trust-through-verified-quality thesis plus its advertised install path (MANIFESTO.md:200) actively support fixing first-run diagnostics. One material risk in the proposed doctor fix: the shipped hook's matcher "Bash(git commit*)" (hooks/hooks.json) is permission-rule syntax, not a valid PreToolUse tool-name matcher, so the hook plausibly never fires — switching the check to "hooks/hooks.json exists" could turn a false WARN into a false PASS over a dead hook.
verified_recommendations.refinement: Keep all three actions but correct the rationale and add one verification step. (a) Cut 2.8.1 framed as a distribution/cache resync, not a repo correction: HEAD already has the right plugin.json and doctor template; the bump exists to give the post-release fixes (f72bb03, a769bf2) a distinct version so version-keyed installs/caches (including the maintainer's own stale 2.7.0-manifest cache at ~/.claude/plugins/cache/dmokong-plugins/speculator/2.8.0/) pick them up. Add a CHANGELOG 2.8.1 entry and consider git tags going forward so a version label maps to exactly one commit. (b) Before changing the doctor check to "hooks/hooks.json exists", verify the PreToolUse hook actually fires on a real `git commit` — the matcher "Bash(git commit*)" is likely invalid PreToolUse syntax (matchers match tool names like "Bash"); if dead, fix the matcher to "Bash" (with the git-commit filtering done in the prompt, which already describes its own scope) in the same 2.8.1 release, otherwise the new check reports PASS over a non-functioning hook. Also delete the --fix instruction at sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:180 that writes the hook into settings.local.json (duplicate registration). (c) Fix README.md:34 by dropping "and register the pre-commit hook" and noting the hook auto-registers with the plugin. Finally, restate the new-user impact honestly: a fresh install today gets the false warning but NOT the version lie; the version lie affects installs made between 2026-04-16 and 2026-04-28 and any cache not refreshed since.

verified_recommendations.rec: Publish a cost/latency model and a documented 'lite profile': a README section stating typical judge dispatches and wall-clock per phase, plus a minimal-gates configuration for small changes, and generalize ROADMAP's risk_level-conditional gate enablement beyond Gate 2c — today a Max-subscription user has no way to reason about what /spec run costs.
verified_recommendations.reasoning:
The core premise is verified: grep across README.md, MANIFESTO.md, ROADMAP.md, CHANGELOG.md, and docs/ finds zero cost/latency documentation — the sole latency mention in the repo is ROADMAP.md:110 (Gate 2c risk note). The 594-line README never tells an adopter that /spec run dispatches the spec-scorer agent 1-4 times (self-improvement loop, max_spec_retries: 3 per README:412), the code-reviewer 1-2 times (re-dispatch after self-fix, skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-review.md:12), up to 3 Gate 2 test-fix cycles (max_code_retries), the spec-compactor at close, and opt-in scorers each with up to 3 retries. For a Max-subscription user the currency is usage-limit consumption and wall-clock, and the README gives no handle on either. That documentation gap is real and cheap to fix because every retry cap is already a documented config value.

However, three of the recommendation's specifics fail against the repo. (1) "Wall-clock per phase" is unmaintainable guesswork: run time is dominated by the implementation phase (subagent-driven-development), which varies by orders of magnitude with feature scope; ROADMAP item #6 (Calibration Guide, lines 167-178) already plans exactly this kind of published tuning guidance and explicitly gates it on Spec-Bench data ("Status: Spec-Bench data needed first") — publishing unmeasured numbers now would contradict the project's measurement-first thesis, which is its entire brand. (2) The "lite profile" half-exists and is documented: opt-in gates 2a/2b default to off (README:424-453; the doctor --init template ships required-gates-only per sdlc-doctor/SKILL.md:202-214), and MANIFESTO.md:119 makes graduated rigor an explicit design principle. What does not exist is skipping the four required gates — sdlc-run/SKILL.md:69-77 hardcodes gates 1/2/3/4 evidence files with enabled-conditionals only for 2a/2b, so the required: true config keys have no orchestrator skip path. A true "minimal-gates configuration for small changes" is therefore an orchestrator feature, not a README section, and it tensions with the anti-dark-code direction (ROADMAP's "Deliberately deferred" section even refuses auto-merge at Full Auto + low risk — the maintainer consistently resists weakening required controls). The honest answer for small changes is "don't route them through /spec run," which is itself an undocumented gap. (3) "Generalize ROADMAP's risk_level-conditional gate enablement beyond Gate 2c" misreads the ROADMAP: backlog line 185 already states the generalized version ("Today opt-in gates are global on/off via sdlc.local.md. Binding Gate 2c to risk_level: high (and similar)...") and line 110 explicitly defers it to v3. Gate 2c itself is in active development right now (untracked agents/comprehension-scorer/AGENT.md and skills/sdlc-run/references/phase-comprehension.md in the working tree, using a global gates.comprehension.enabled flag) — pulling the generalization forward duplicates an existing backlog item and competes with the declared #1 priority for a solo maintainer.

Net: the transparency problem exists and the dispatch-count portion is low-effort/high-value, but roughly two-thirds of the recommendation is either already planned (and deliberately sequenced behind data), already half-shipped (opt-in defaults ARE the lite profile), or based on a misreading of ROADMAP.
verified_recommendations.refinement: Keep the cheap, durable kernel and drop the rest. (1) Add a README "What a run costs" subsection under the /spec run section that enumerates agent dispatches per phase with their retry caps — all derivable from existing config (spec-scorer x1 + up to 3 self-improvement re-scores; code-reviewer x1 + 1 re-dispatch after self-fix; up to 3 Gate 2 test-fix cycles; spec-compactor x1 at close; each opt-in gate adds one scorer agent with up to 3 retries). Dispatch counts are stable across model generations; wall-clock numbers are not — omit them, or give only order-of-magnitude framing ("a Full Auto run is tens of agent invocations, not a few"). PREMISES VERIFIED:
1. Scorer model unpinned at exactly the cited location. /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/benchmarks/src/spec_bench/scoring.py:196-201 runs `[claude, "-p", prompt, "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]` with no `--model` flag — the spec scorer uses whatever the user's CLI default happens to be. The published 3-round results header asserts "scorer_model: claude-sonnet-4-6" — a claim the code cannot guarantee.
2. Benchmark scoring prompt is not the production rubric. scoring.py's SCORING_PROMPT_TEMPLATE (lines 56-106) is a ~50-line abbreviated prompt with generic 3-band anchors. The production rubric /Users/dustincheng/projects/speculator/rubrics/spec-quality.md is 346 lines with anti-inflation guidance and per-dimension calibration examples. Benchmark spec scores are therefore not produced by the production instrument they claim to validate.
3. "Zero valid datapoints" is accurate. The only end-to-end run (benchmarks/runs/bench-2026-03-28-001, a single-target smoke run — never the 8-target default.yml matrix) used the pre-Option-C harness whose assertions were misaligned with requirements: F03 "Save multiple named routes" fails with "No 'Feels like' text found"; F10 "Transfer risk indicator" fails with "No time-of-day greeting found" (both functional-results.yml files). The Option C harness (mock_data.py, app_navigator.py, vision_judge.py, wired into review.py:224) was built AFTER that run (git: ceee8b2 implementation precedes 8c1d75a/e90fe66 harness commits) and has never produced an outcome datapoint — runs/ contains nothing post-Option-C.
4. "One contradicting datapoint" is essentially accurate. In that smoke run, spec score went 7.0 → 8.3 (+1.3, iteration-log.yml) yet functional outcomes were identical: 12/20 vs 12/20. The LLM-judge comparison was never even completed — judge-scorecard.yml exists only for the original arm (7.7). Strictly it's a null result via an invalid instrument, but the reviewer's framing is fair.
5. The circularity critique is real. The published evidence (results/3-round-spec-quality.yml, 18 datapoints, "F4: Speculator ALWAYS improves specs") measures only spec scores issued by the same scorer that supplied the improvement feedback — no implementation was ever built. The MANIFESTO's "result that convinced me this works" (SPEC-011, 7.7→8.4) is likewise spec-score-only. No no-feedback control arm exists anywhere — iterate_spec (scoring.py:271+) always conditions regeneration on scorecard feedback.
6. The experiment is the harness's stated purpose. benchmarks/README.md's first sentence: spec-bench "tests whether spec quality predicts implementation quality." That test has never validly run.

WHERE THE RECOMMENDATION OVERREACHES:
A. "Before any further gate-building" conflicts with ROADMAP.md's explicit #1 priority, Gate 2c (Comprehension Gate, "top priority"). Gate 2c targets a different failure mode (dark code / comprehension) whose justification rests on the NBJ three-fix framework, not on the spec-score→outcome correlation. Holding it hostage to this experiment is a sequencing overreach — though the spirit aligns with the roadmap's own language that empirical validation is "the single highest-leverage research move" (priority #2) and with the MANIFESTO's "weights will be tuned by real-world data."
B. The full 8-target matrix is over-scoped for a solo maintainer: 4 of 8 targets require copilot-cli + gpt-4.1 access, and the harness/model axes test spec-generator diversity, not the score→outcome link. With runs_per_combination: 1 and observed judge noise (raw-score sigma 0.06–0.46 in the 3-round data), 8 single-shot pairs plus controls (~24 full app implementations) is heavy compute for statistically thin inference.

verified_recommendations.reasoning:
The recommendation's general premise is verified: Speculator has zero deterministic verification anywhere in the pipeline. Every gate — including Gate 4, which rubrics/evidence-package.md line 3 explicitly defines as "a mechanical completeness checklist... no subjective scoring — every check is binary pass/fail" — is executed by an LLM following markdown instructions (skills/gate-check/SKILL.md), and the only hook (hooks/hooks.json) is a warning-only prompt hook. The repo has no scripts/ directory. This gap has bitten once for real: claudeclaw's agentic-sdlc-extraction gate-4-summary.yml asserts gate_2 and gate_3 "pass" in prose while NO gate-2-quality.yml or gate-3-review.yml artifacts exist — exactly what a mechanized Gate 4 check would catch.

However, three of the recommendation's specific claims fail adversarial checking. (1) Its motivating example is wrong: the SPEC-001 "vacuous Gate 2 pass" (speculator/docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-2-quality.yml) is honest, schema-clean, arithmetically correct evidence — the 25 secrets-scan tests genuinely pass, and the file documents the markdown-only rationale. Re-running the tests reproduces the pass. The vacuity is a gate-semantics policy gap, already diagnosed as C8 in docs/REVIEW-2026-04-28.md, with a documented project-chosen fix (ROADMAP.md backlog line 193: an N/A-rationale paragraph in rubrics/code-quality.md — "do not invent fragile LLM tests"). A deterministic verifier would rubber-stamp SPEC-001, so the script does NOT close the hole it cites. The companion "diff must add test files when Gate 2 claims feature tests" rule either never fires on SPEC-001 (it didn't claim feature tests) or, if interpreted loosely, conflicts with the project's documented prompt-only-spec stance — and most of Speculator's own specs are markdown-only. (2) "Catches LLM arithmetic errors": empirically recomputing all 28 fully-parseable claudeclaw gate-1 scorecards against the configured weights found ZERO true arithmetic errors — the 3 mismatches (8.3 vs 8.25; 8.4 vs 8.35 twice) are rounding-convention ambiguities (rubric only says "round to one decimal place"), none outcome-changing; a naive verifier would emit false failures on exactly these boundary cases. (3) "Re-run the recorded test command": real recorded commands include `cd ~/projects/r2mcp && npm test` (a different repo), DB-dependent vitest invocations, "manual end-to-end shell execution", and "N/A" — re-execution is slow, side-effectful, env-dependent, often impossible, and executing arbitrary YAML-recorded shell strings from a script is its own hazard. Also note the speculator-repo gate-2 evidence format doesn't even record a command field (only the consumer project's newer files do), and evidence schema heterogeneity across plugin versions is severe (11+ scorecards deviate), so "validate evidence YAML schema" requires schema versioning or it fails all historical evidence.

Direction check: no conflict with MANIFESTO.md (LLM-as-judge for *scoring* is the thesis; arithmetic/file-existence verification isn't judging). ROADMAP.md's deliberate deferral of "structured schemas" applies to rubrics-as-prompts, not evidence YAML, and its backlog (Makefile, CI workflow, hard-block hook mode) shows deterministic infrastructure fits the house style (the repo already maintains 122 deterministic tests). But the script is not on the roadmap, whose #1 priority is Gate 2c, and the enforcement gap remains: the script would be invoked by the same LLM that writes the evidence, so it narrows rather than closes self-reporting.

Net: the cheap subset (mechanize Gate 4's already-binary checklist, recompute overall/threshold/dimension-minimum with a pinned rounding rule, weights-sum lint) is genuinely worth ~a day of a solo maintainer's time and would have caught the one real incident found. The expensive subset (test re-execution, diff-must-add-tests) is fragile, mis-motivated, and partly contradicts the project's documented direction. Hence partially-sound, not sound — and not refuted, because the core gap is real, file-verified, and has a real-world failure instance.
verified_recommendations.refinement: Scope the script down to what is deterministic-by-nature and demonstrably failing today: (1) Mechanize Gate 4 — implement rubrics/evidence-package.md's existing binary checklist as the script: evidence-file existence per required gate, result fields, blocking-flag presence, spec frontmatter status. This is faithful codification of stated design (the rubric already says "mechanical... no subjective scoring") and would have caught the real agentic-sdlc-extraction incident (gate-4 pass with missing gate-2/3 artifacts). (2) Recompute weighted overall + threshold + per-dimension-minimum for gate-1/2a/2b scorecards, reading weights from .claude/sdlc.local.md frontmatter (already machine-readable YAML), with a pinned rounding convention (amend rubrics/spec-quality.md line 287 to specify half-up) and a ±0.05 tolerance to avoid false failures on the 8.25/8.35 boundary cases found in production evidence. verified_recommendations.rec: Size trust-ladder bands to measured noise: score each spec N=3 times (or at minimum measure test-retest sigma on a fixed spec corpus once), use the median, and require the score to clear full_auto_threshold by more than the measured sigma before granting Full Auto. Simultaneously blind the scorer to the pass threshold (have the orchestrator, not the scorer agent, apply threshold/minimum logic) to remove score-attraction bias at the 7.8/8.0 boundary.
verified_recommendations.reasoning:
GROUNDING CHECK (file evidence):

1. Premise "bands not sized to measured noise" — TRUE. The 7.8 full_auto / 8.0 self_improvement gap (skills/sdlc-run/SKILL.md lines 95-112, .claude/sdlc.local.md run: block) is justified philosophically ("you don't skip the gym" — MANIFESTO.md Section V), never empirically. No test-retest sigma exists anywhere in the repo. The only variance data, benchmarks/results/3-round-spec-quality.yml (sigma_raw 0.06-0.46, sigma_iterated 0.06-0.40), conflates spec-GENERATION variance with scorer variance — each round scored a fresh spec, so pure scorer noise is unmeasured. A single-sample LLM-judge score driving an autonomy decision with a 0.2-point band gap is a legitimate concern in principle.

2. Premise "blind the scorer to the pass threshold; have the orchestrator apply threshold logic" — MISIDENTIFIES THE ARCHITECTURE. The 7.8/8.0 trust-ladder logic the reviewer names is ALREADY applied by the orchestrator, not the scorer: sdlc-run/SKILL.md Section 3 + references/phase-scoring.md ("Read the scorecard and extract overall... Record Trust Decision"). The scorer (agents/spec-scorer/AGENT.md steps 4+7) applies only the Gate-1 pass threshold, which is 7.0 — not 7.8/8.0 (scorecard at docs/specs/eval-quality-scoring/evidence/gate-1-scorecard.yml: threshold: 7.0). The separation the reviewer demands already exists at the exact boundary they cite. The residual leak is real but indirect: the scorer is handed the whole .claude/sdlc.local.md, whose run: block contains 7.8/8.0, so context contamination is possible — but the scorer never applies those numbers.

3. Premise "score-attraction bias at the 7.8/8.0 boundary" — UNSUPPORTED BY THE REPO'S OWN DATA. Benchmark iterated scores cluster at 8.1-8.8 (avg 8.4-8.6, finding F5), the sole in-repo dogfooded scorecard is 8.0, and ClaudeClaw production runs scored 8.3-8.8 — no clustering at 7.8. If there's an attraction artifact, it's compression toward ~8.4 (rubric-band anchoring), which blinding the threshold would not fix.

4. Severity of the problem — OVERSTATED. Hard gates bound the blast radius of score noise: risk_level high/critical → forced Guided; intent_verifiability < 8 → forced Guided; any blocking flag → fail (SKILL.md lines 96-99, AGENT.md rules). A noise-driven false Full Auto can only occur on a low/medium-risk spec with intent_verifiability ≥ 8 and zero blocking flags. Additionally, scores in [7.8, 8.0) already get a second sample by design — they enter the self-improvement loop and are rescored before any Full Auto grant.

COST/BENEFIT for a solo maintainer:
- N=3 median on EVERY scoring event is a bad default trade: the self-improvement loop rescores up to max_spec_retries=3 times, so a single pipeline run could need up to 12 scorer dispatches (sonnet agent reading spec+rubric+config each time). Latency/token cost is real; the marginal risk it mitigates is already bounded by hard gates.
- The sigma-margin requirement is functionally just "raise full_auto_threshold" — a knob that already exists in config and that the maintainer already uses (ClaudeClaw tightened full_auto to 8.3).
- The blinding refactor touches the agent prompt, scorecard-template.yml (threshold/result fields), spec-score skill, gate-check, and 3 sibling scorer agents for consistency — and since the orchestrator and skills are ALSO LLMs (lib/ contains only markdown; there is no deterministic scoring runtime), moving pass/fail from one LLM to another buys no determinism. The honest version of this fix is a deterministic script/hook computing weighted-overall + pass/fail, which the recommendation doesn't propose.

DIRECTION CHECK: No conflict with ROADMAP.md's deliberately-deferred list or MANIFESTO.md. Empirical judge validation is actually IN the stated direction (ROADMAP #2 mutation-testing validation, #6 calibration guide "Spec-Bench data needed first", existing spec-bench calibrate human-vs-judge protocol, and compute_variance_analysis already in benchmarks/src/spec_bench/report.py). But the declared #1 priority is Gate 2c; standing N=3 machinery competes with that critical path. MANIFESTO's "the score isn't the point, the improvement is" cuts both ways: it concedes scores are directional (supporting the noise concern) while arguing against engineering false precision at the boundary.

VERDICT: partially-sound. The cheap kernel — measure test-retest sigma once on a fixed corpus and size the band accordingly — is sound, roadmap-aligned, and nearly free given the existing harness. The standing N=3-per-spec requirement is a poor trade, and the blinding rationale rests on a misread of where threshold logic lives plus a bias that the repo's own score distribution does not exhibit.
verified_recommendations.refinement: Keep the minimum-viable version, drop the rest: (1) Run a ONE-TIME test-retest study using the existing spec-bench machinery (benchmarks/src/spec_bench/report.py already has compute_variance_analysis) — score 3-5 fixed specs ~5x each with the unchanged spec-scorer, publish sigma in benchmarks/results/, and feed it into the already-planned calibration guide (ROADMAP item #6). (2) If measured sigma exceeds the 0.2 band gap, widen the gap using the existing config knobs (raise run.full_auto_threshold in sdlc.local.md) — no new median machinery; only adopt per-spec N=3 if sigma proves larger than the gap AND boundary scores are actually observed in practice (current evidence shows real runs landing 8.3+). (3) For threshold leakage, do the minimal fix: stop handing the scorer the full .claude/sdlc.local.md — pass only scoring weights and dimension_minimum, and have the invoking spec-score skill (or better, a deterministic hook script) compute weighted-overall and stamp threshold:/result: into the scorecard. Do not restructure the trust ladder — the orchestrator already owns the 7.8/8.0 logic (sdlc-run/SKILL.md + references/phase-scoring.md), so that half of the recommendation is already implemented.
